item.title: [Lex Clips] Why the Roman Empire collapsed in the East | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反常识的观察：拜占庭其实是"抱怨驱动型"的稳定系统

大多数人以为帝国稳固要靠肃杀与统一口径，而这段对话揭示的却是相反的图景——东罗马之所以能延续千年，恰恰是因为它把"抱怨"制度化了。老百姓一直在抱怨税收，皇帝知道抱怨没有惩罚成本，抱怨甚至能换来让步。抱怨从"体制的裂缝"变成了"体制的减压阀"。

这在系统论上有一个深刻的类比：现代分布式系统的自愈能力，不是来自"消灭故障"，而是来自"允许小故障被观察到"。Netflix 的 Chaos Monkey 就是主动制造故障来验证系统健康。拜占庭的持续抱怨，就是它自己养出来的 Chaos Monkey——让不满在低烈度下被表达、被吸收、被回应，从而永远不会积累到"分离主义"那个临界点。这也许可以解释一个让 Kaldellis 自己都惊讶的数据：1200 年里只有保加利亚一个地区真的想脱离。一个健康的抱怨文化，比一支忠诚的军队更能防止分裂。

跨域连接：Tamerlane 是一个"负熵事件"，也是历史学最少被谈论的黑天鹅

对话里一笔带过的细节其实惊人：帖木儿从中亚横扫过来，把奥斯曼打散，意外地给拜占庭多续了 50 年命。这在演化生物学里有一个精确的对应概念——"捕食者的捕食者"（apex predator suppression）。在生态系统里，狼被消灭会让鹿泛滥，但如果狼有自己的天敌，中间层物种反而能获得更长的稳定期。

拜占庭之所以能到 1453 而不是 1400 灭亡，不是因为它变强了，而是因为"吃它的那位"被更凶的一位咬了一口。这引出了一个让人不适的历史观：很多我们归因于"文明韧性"的延续，其实只是别人打架时溅出的时间红利。 把这个视角推到今天——我们今天所谓的"和平红利"，有多少是结构性的胜利，有多少只是我们尚未看清的"帖木儿之战"在遥远的地方替我们腾出的时间？

修辞的秘密：不是"说得好"，而是"信得深"——从亚里士多德到现代品牌学

Kaldellis 借院长发预算通知的例子讲了一件微妙的事：同样的行动，包装在不同的信念结构里，会产生完全不同的服从与共识。而 Lex 追问的那句才是命门——"你必须真的相信它"。

这里有一个几乎没有人指出的跨领域联系：现代信号理论（signaling theory）里的"高代价信号"和亚里士多德给僭主的建议在结构上是同一件事。亚里士多德说："想让人以为你是仁君？最省事的办法就是真的做一个仁君。" 因为伪装仁慈的边际成本会随时间递增（要撒的谎越来越多、越来越难圆），而真正的仁慈是"零维护成本"的信号。拜占庭皇帝之所以让"我在为你们统治"这句话有效千年，是因为说这句话的成本，被他们真的做过的事情摊薄了——像一笔千年期的信任摊销。

由此我看到一条更让人意外的隐藏线索：东罗马的真正遗产不是版图、不是法律、不是东正教，而是"充分同意（sufficient consent）"这种被低估的政治工艺。它不追求人民爱戴，只追求"替代方案更糟"这一底线感受被牢牢维持。这一心理契约比任何军事同盟都难被外部势力撬动——直到有一天，外部势力大到你连"是否留下"这个问题都不必再问。这或许才是所有长寿组织（无论帝国、宗教、还是苹果这样的公司）的隐秘公分母：它们从不承诺天堂，它们只承诺你离开会更糟。

📝 转录文本

When did the decline begin?

Q: When did the decline of the East Roman Empire begin for you? Throughout this loops, when — if you were to predict — would you be able to say where this is going to be over? And what led to that decline and the collapse? So, of course, the collapse is finally in 1453 AD.

A: That's actually a very good question. In other words, not looking at all of the previous crises that caused loss of territory — like, you know, you lose Egypt, you lose Syria, you lose Palestine, you lose Eastern Asia Minor — but at what point could you say there's no coming back from this?

I would say early 14th century. Somewhere between 1300 and 1350, the sources of resilience begin to dry up. And you have a number of setbacks. They lose Asia Minor to the Turks by 1300, roughly. Which means that you can now no longer draw upon the two wings, as it were — the European side and the Asian side, which they could always do. They're confined to Europe. And then you have a series of civil wars. You have the Serbian expansion. And you have the Black Death. And they never recover from that.

1300: a small state, but not yet doomed

In 1300, you look at it and you think that's a tiny little Balkan state. Okay, it goes from Constantinople to the Adriatic in this sort of ribbon — this corridor that just stretches across the Balkans. And yet, it's not that much smaller than England. And has about as much, if not more, revenue because they're still able to raise cash through taxes. So the emperor is still able to pull in a lot of gold. And his territory is not completely insignificant. It's not what the Roman Empire used to be, but I don't think that it's completely lost.

Then they have some civil wars. Then one rebel makes a deal with the Serbs, surrenders half that territory. Okay — after that, game over.

The Tamerlane accident

What's surprising is it takes the Turks that long. 1453 — that's another century. It didn't need to be. It looks like they might have had it wrapped up like 50 years after — you know, by 1400. But then accidental events happened. This is Timur — Tamerlane — comes from Central Asia, trounces the Turks, breaks up their empire. So they go into a cycle of rebuilding. And so that's why it takes another 50 years. Anyway. But I'd say by the 1340s, game over.

Why did it collapse?

Q: And so, why did it collapse? What are the things on the ground when you zoom out — the thing that ate away at the… I like this term, sources of resilience.

A: Oh, foreign invasions. There's no question.

Q: It's just invasions.

A: It's foreign invasions — those which they could not cope with. They coped with lots of them. But all it takes is to lose to one and you lose some part of your territory. And if you keep losing enough, you won't have anything to fall back on. And that's what happens.

Could it have lasted another thousand years?

Q: So really, if there's no significant external invasions, the Eastern Roman Empire — the fundamentals, the taxation system, the way the policies were developed, the way the politics was done, the way the representation was done, all of that — this empire could have lasted for another thousand years?

A: Absolutely. I'm absolutely convinced of this. Because you never see movements to split away — that is, there's no separatist movements, at least not on the part of any Roman provincials.

There's one separatist movement, which is Bulgaria. You mentioned Bulgaria was conquered, right, in 1018. There's a couple of independentist movements, rebellions — eventually one succeeds in 1185. So just under two centuries. So Bulgaria is the only part of this empire that actively seeks to break away and does so.

But for the rest of the 1200 years, you don't find provincials wanting to leave this system. However much they complain about the taxes, they don't want to leave and they make no move to do so.

You also never have a moment when the center loses its ability to tax its territories. You don't have widespread peasant uprisings or agricultural rebellions or these kinds of things — which happens in every other one of these empires. They also never decide to partition it. The Franks partition theirs, right, after Charlemagne. No — there are no Roman warlords that try to carve out a piece of the territory for themselves and rule it independently. Nope, that doesn't happen either.

So if you're looking at the kinds of factors that cause states to fail, fragment, become ungovernable — these never happen.

The most dangerous neighborhood in the world

Q: This is so incredible. This is truly incredible. In the history of human civilization, just an incredible study of what human societies can form. So what are the whys? Why? Why was it so stable?

A: Well, now look — there are other stable… like for example the British monarchy. That's been around for quite a while. States like Portugal, Japan — they have one thing in common. They're all tucked away at the edges. Like they're off on their little islands or whatever. They're not like…

We're talking about a society that was right in the middle of one of the main corridors of empire building and new religions in the world. This is the most dangerous neighborhood that you can possibly live in. So no wonder it didn't survive. It would have been extraordinary if it had. But absent those forces, I don't see any forces of internal decomposition that would have imperiled it significantly.

Why no internal decomposition?

Q: So — and we've talked about a lot of it, but just returning to it — why was there no internal sources of decomposition? Like why did it work so well? Why did the people feel represented? Yes, they… by the way, you talked about how they complain about the taxes, which is one of the signs of a healthy society. It's like complaining about the weather. The fact that you complain about the taxes actually means that you're happy with the taxes, kind of counterintuitively. It's not too bad. Your complaining is part of the system.

Anyway — but the why, why, why did it work so well? Why did everybody get along? Feel represented? No, not get along — not in a silly way. I mean, sufficiently to where warlords wouldn't rise and break apart the system, and if there were those kinds of forces, they would come back together. Why is it such a great self-healing system?

A: So — you're exactly right. So sufficient is the key word. We don't want to idealize this. They're not just dancing in the streets holding hands. So even when it comes to the tax system, they are complaining all the time. Of course, they're doing so because they know that complaining gets you things. There's no disincentive to complaining. You might not get what you want, or you might get it, but no harm will come to you. So they're complaining all the time. They have all of these civil wars.

A two-part answer: persuasion + identity

I think I would give you a two-part answer, so really quickly, as to why this held together so well.

First is that an extraordinary effort was made by the authorities to persuade their subjects that they were ruling on their behalf. And I think for the most part they actually tended to do so.

Second is that it had a very tightly unified identity as Roman and as Orthodox. In other words, for the most time they knew that they were surrounded by enemies who were not those things. And they did not want to live under the power of non-Romans and non-Christian people.

And so those two factors combined, I think, gave everybody reason to hold it together. The alternatives were worse.

The power of rhetoric — and the necessity of belief

Q: Yeah, that's a really brilliant summary. And I think we should mention that both the rhetoric and the action are important. I think you've articulated that rhetoric is really powerful — stating over and over and over that you represent the people, that you're acting on behalf of the people, and doing the action. But it's not enough to actually do the action. You would think that's all — Machiavelli says that's the only thing that matters. Then, no — the rhetoric actually has a lot of power because it is a uniting idea and ideal that propagates through time, and it pacifies you. It makes you… it puts you at peace. Rhetoric is important.

A: I'll give you an example. As an academic administrator — I was a chair of a department for years and years — there's a difference between a dean who says, "Sorry, bad budget situation, we're just going to have to cut your programs," on the one hand, and a dean who says, "I am fully on board with what you're trying to accomplish with these programs. I am a defender of the humanities. Unfortunately, we have this budget situation and we're going to have to cut some of the programs, but I assure you — I'm in this with you, together — the moment we find the funds, we will restore them."

Those two rhetorical strategies — aiming at the same practical outcome — will produce very different levels of compliance and consensus.

Rhetoric only works if you actually believe it

Q: And I should say that — there's subtlety there. The best rhetoric in that context, and maybe in the East Roman Empire, is: you also have to believe it.

A: Yes. It has to be credible. You can't just say it and then not do it.

Q: One of the things I've learned about humans, just doing this little podcast, is that the best way to say a powerful thing is to really believe it. And so — this is clearly one of the things that made the Roman Empire work: doing the action is the best way to make the rhetoric powerful and effective.

Aristotle's advice to tyrants

A: Yeah. This is sort of what Aristotle says about tyrants. In the Politics, Aristotle's giving essentially advice to tyrants on how to protect themselves — because tyrants are usually hated and are killed in coups. And he says: essentially, how do you manage to secure yourself when you're a tyrant?

Well, you can start by maybe obeying the laws, or making sure that your subjects obey the laws, and maybe work for the interest of your… essentially, he's telling them to kind of become benevolent kings. And the best way to persuade your subjects that you're legitimate benevolent kings is to be that.

Yeah.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反常识的观察：拜占庭其实是"抱怨驱动型"的稳定系统
大多数人以为帝国稳固要靠肃杀与统一口径，而这段对话揭示的却是相反的图景——**东罗马之所以能延续千年，恰恰是因为它把"抱怨"制度化了**。老百姓一直在抱怨税收，皇帝知道抱怨没有惩罚成本，抱怨甚至能换来让步。抱怨从"体制的裂缝"变成了"体制的减压阀"。
这在系统论上有一个深刻的类比：**现代分布式系统的自愈能力，不是来自"消灭故障"，而是来自"允许小故障被观察到"**。Netflix 的 Chaos Monkey 就是主动制造故障来验证系统健康。拜占庭的持续抱怨，就是它自己养出来的 Chaos Monkey——让不满在低烈度下被表达、被吸收、被回应，从而永远不会积累到"分离主义"那个临界点。这也许可以解释一个让 Kaldellis 自己都惊讶的数据：1200 年里只有保加利亚一个地区真的想脱离。**一个健康的抱怨文化，比一支忠诚的军队更能防止分裂。**
## 跨域连接：Tamerlane 是一个"负熵事件"，也是历史学最少被谈论的黑天鹅
对话里一笔带过的细节其实惊人：帖木儿从中亚横扫过来，把奥斯曼打散，**意外地给拜占

item.title: [a16z] Software in the Age of Agents | The a16z Show
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：Agent 不是软件的杀手，而是软件的"哈勃望远镜"

大多数人在讨论"headless software"时，都把目光聚焦在UI 死了这件事上。但视频中真正令人震撼的洞察藏在 Steven 一句几乎被忽略的话里——他说"agent 其实就是一个'跑很久但可能永远跑不完的程序'，我们过去把这叫 bug，现在叫 feature"。

这句话像一把撬棍，撬开了整个 SaaS 讨论的地基：agent 的真正革命性不在于"替代人"，而在于把企业内部第一次可以承受"长时间、非确定性、可反复迭代"的计算过程。这意味着我们第一次能"看见"那些原本因为算力太贵、注意力太稀缺而被压抑的业务问题——就像哈勃望远镜第一次让人类看见深空一样。

所以"software losing its head"这个命题反过来才成立：软件不是失去了头，而是第一次长出了会做梦的大脑。UI 的消失是表象，真正在发生的是"企业认知深度"的解锁。

跨领域连接：企业软件的"暗物质"与人类学中的"隐性知识"

Sema 反复提到的"context graph"、"exceptions"、"in someone's head"，其实和人类学家 Michael Polanyi 半个世纪前提出的tacit knowledge（隐性知识）是同一件事。Polanyi 有一句著名的话："We know more than we can tell."（我们知道的比我们能说出的多。）

有趣的连接在这里：SAP、Salesforce 这类看似臃肿的系统之所以无法被 vibe code 替代，并不是因为它们的代码有多神秘，而是因为它们是"企业隐性知识的化石层"。每一个奇怪的字段、每一个定制的工作流，都是一位早已离职的员工在某个下午做的一次微小决策的沉积岩。

这也解释了为什么 Amazon 的"默认偏向客户"策略如此革命性——他们不是在优化客服，他们在做一件人类学从未做到的事：把隐性知识用 LLM 大规模显性化。这和"人类学转录田野笔记"的过程惊人相似，只是 Amazon 的田野是整个消费社会。

更进一步的跨界联想：这个逻辑同样适用于医学、法律甚至外交。radiologist（放射科医生）不是被 AI 替代了，而是需求被放大了——因为"能看见的更多了"，所以"值得看的也更多了"。这是Jevons 悖论在认知劳动上的重演：效率提升不会减少需求，只会释放被压抑的需求。

一个让人后背发凉的预测：真正的护城河将从"数据"迁移到"例外处理的语料库"

大家都在谈"data is the new oil"，但视频里藏着一个更狠的洞察：未来企业最值钱的资产，可能不是他们的结构化数据，而是他们的"例外处理档案库"——那些没有被 Salesforce 字段捕捉、只存在于销售总监脑子里的、"如果客户在亚洲，我们这样回，如果在美国，我们那样回"的判断链条。

这里有一个几乎没人指出的悖论：agent 越强大，"边缘案例"就越有价值，因为默认路径已经被商品化了。就像自动驾驶越成熟，真正难的不是 95% 的高速路况，而是 5% 的施工路段——那 5% 才是特斯拉和 Waymo 之间的鸿沟。

所以对创业者的真正启示是反 Salesforce 的：不要去建"更好的系统 of record"，而是去建"系统 of exceptions"——一个专门捕获、结构化、并可复用企业内部所有"例外判断"的层。语音代理、屏幕录制、会议转录……这些工具的真正价值不在于"节省时间"，而在于它们是人类隐性知识的 CT 扫描仪。

而更远处的一个大胆预测：十年后，最有价值的企业收购标的可能不是"有多少 ARR"，而是"有多少高质量的例外语料"。这就像今天的 AI 训练数据市场——原始互联网数据几乎无价值，但人类反馈标注数据可以卖天价。企业内部的隐性知识，就是下一个 RLHF 语料池。

最后一个意外洞察：中间件的诅咒是一条永恒的物理定律

Steven 提到"middleware layer 永远不稳定"——这句话表面是在讲 MCP，但它其实揭示了一条跨越 40 年计算史的元规律：任何试图"抽象掉别人"的层，最终都会被"被抽象的那一方"吸收或消灭。

这条规律和生态学中的"关键种（keystone species）"理论惊人一致：生态系统中，越是"中间层"的物种，越容易在环境剧变时灭绝；顶端捕食者和底层生产者反而最稳定。

在企业软件里，底层（数据+业务逻辑=SAP）和顶端（agent+用户意图）都会活得很好，真正危险的是那些"我来做个统一 API 层"的创业公司——他们既没有底层的深度嵌入，也没有顶端的意图理解，正好是生态崩溃时第一批消失的物种。

这也是为什么 a16z 的投资建议如此清晰："aim for the middle between two established players"——不是做中间件，而是做"两个功能之间的翻译层"。翻译层和中间件的区别在于：翻译层创造新语义，中间件只搬运旧语义。这是一个语言学层面的差别，却决定了商业上的生与死。

📝 转录文本

开场：软件的"头"正在消失？

主持人（旁白式引入）： There are many things that made software sticky, but a lot of it had to do with the fact that the way a human interacts. In an agentic world, do you actually need that? The data, the logic, everything stored below it is really where the value is.

There's this wild underestimation about like you could vibe code your way into enterprise software.

Larry Ellison at Oracle, he went on a rant about how enterprise software was so stupid because everybody customized it. The minute you automate the most mundane thing and think you have it all squared away, whole new things appear.

Misconception right now is that you can just have you know Postgres database and APIs and then bam like you can replace SAP. That's like absolutely not true. That piece around the logic and everything else that is encaptured in SAP is way way more important than the fact that like oh this data just happens to be in this database.

One of the things that happens in technology shifts is nobody understands exponential when it's happening. The biggest opportunity right now is —

嘉宾介绍

Welcome to the A16Z podcast. I'm here with Sema Amble, a partner here on the enterprise team, and Steven Sinofsky, who is a board partner at A16Z, as well as a former member of Microsoft, friend of the firm.

Um, and here we are today to talk about a piece that Sema wrote about a month ago called Is Software Losing Its Head? And this piece, I'll let Sema talk about it in her own words. But this piece was written a couple months ago. Salesforce announced that they would be going headless. Today we're here to kind of discuss, you know, what does that mean? What does that mean for the future of SaaS products, the future of software more generally?

什么是 Headless Software？

Sema： So headless software — it's not a new term, but I think has really risen in the public domain of interest, a topic that people are talking about. One of the interesting news points has been Salesforce making this announcement. They were launching Headless 360 which was really, in classic Salesforce motion history, a marketing announcement more than anything else.

But it does capture — it's an acknowledgement of what's happening, which is: traditional software had been built around humans accessing it. And it was workflow to capture data. We could talk more about what that meant. In an agentic world, do you actually need that? The UI doesn't matter because the agent isn't accessing the software via the UI. We could unpack whether the UI matters or not.

But the idea of being headless is: the data, the logic, everything stored below it is really where the value is — not just the workflow software that's being tracked at the top.

Salesforce 的公告：品牌重塑还是真变革？

主持人： And this was announced a couple months ago. I'm curious kind of in the past couple months, what have we seen? You wrote in the piece kind of at the beginning you were a little bit funny maybe and you said, is this really even that big of an announcement? Is this sort of a rebrand of APIs that they had kind of already made available?

Sema： So I'll separate it out into the Salesforce context and then the broader context — which I think the broader context is more interesting.

In the Salesforce context, probably not that interesting. I think Salesforce, rightfully again, it was acknowledging a shift that's happening in the market. I don't, from what I could tell, nothing actually changed. Their 360 product was the same APIs that had always been exposed, now rebranded as their 360 product. And APIs have always existed — for a long time have existed.

But I think the broader trend here is: Salesforce among others are thinking about how they build themselves for the agentic world. If an agent needs to access the data in a CRM like Salesforce, how are they doing it? Are they doing it via the UI or are they using the API? Salesforce is saying, "Okay, we know what is changing — that there are agents who are needing to access the data. Let's offer a headless version for them to interact with the data versus going via the UI."

That said, I don't think anything actually changed in the Salesforce context. But Salesforce is in the old one. Another example is Notion has a headless product, and actually I think that makes even more sense. Many users of Salesforce are probably less technically adept, less likely to be building their own agents — although there are many more people who are doing that with Salesforce. Notion users, all things being equal, are more tech-savvy and more agentic as builders. Notion is one of many other companies that is also trying to figure out: what is it that I offer? What APIs do I expose?

I think Stephen will talk more about MCP. And again, I think a lot of this is also getting caught up a little bit in nomenclature — like, okay, what are we calling things? But the broader trend around how agents access systems of record, I think, is the bigger point.

Chatbot 也算 Headless

主持人： Yeah. From my understanding it's also — I mean it could also apply to something as simple as a chatbot. It's not necessarily just an API or an MCP server. Salesforce acquired Slack a couple of years ago and it could be something as simple as you sort of interacting with a CRM via chatbot.

Sema： Totally. I think I read somewhere that there's been like a 300% increase in Slackbot / Slack agent usage. Which is essentially saying that you don't need to log into the Salesforce UI interface to get the data or whatever data it is. All these are agentic ways of accessing it versus the human needing to go in, log the data, or from a read perspective go back and see — okay, here's this opportunity, here's what happened. That interface is less relevant.

定义之惑：Agent 到底是什么？

主持人： And Stephen, do you have anything to add here just on the kind of definitional territory that we're covering right now?

Steven： Well, sir, I mean we're in definitional hell right now. Part of a new wave of technology is you make up a lot of new words for things that you kind of did before. That's just a natural part of technology evolution.

But I actually think it's super important. First you have this "agent," which as far as I can tell right now is also a new word for a program that takes a very long time to run and might not finish. And that's just like a branding — that's the best branding ever — is to call a program that takes a really long time, which we used to call a bug, is now like the coolest new feature ever, and it's just now an agent.

But in seriousness, the most interesting way I think to think of what you're really talking about doing differently between an agent and an API is: what is the agent itself doing?

三种 Agent 行为

  1. Look up — Is it looking something up? That's actually a pretty lightweight thing that all systems are pretty good at. In fact, many of the newly-announced headless agent APIs are just lookup — you basically have a new interface to the old way to look something up, which is a lot more forgiving, a lot less UI goo.

  2. Do something — Then there's "I want to do something," and that's where you get into very interesting issues: if you do something, you have to be impersonating a specific person, you have to have their credentials. Is it another paid seat? Is it the same paid seat? You have all these interesting enterprise software issues that come up if you actually want to cause a change to a system of record.

  3. Analyze — Then there's the third thing which is analyze. Analyze is more than look something up. It's actually look up a bunch of stuff. It often involves multiple systems. And that seems very very tuned to an agent because you're not time-bounded. You can spend energy, iterate. You can route it to different models and get different answers back and compare them. But it's also where hallucination really is a huge issue — because if you're going to analyze something, you actually need a way to verify that every step of that analysis was correct.

So I think it's super interesting and important when you look at headless and agent — which are conflated — you sort of have to figure out what you're talking about, because we're on different places in the evolution, the learning curve, and the deployment of agents relative to sort of that three-way matrix.

什么让软件"粘"？

主持人： I think this is actually a good lead-up into a follow-up question, which is: historically, what has made software sticky and how are agents starting to disrupt that?

Sema： There are many things that made software sticky, but a lot of it had to do with the fact that it was built around the way a human interacts. The UI was sticky because of the number of times you had to read and write, the frequency of access, the downstream workflows, all of the undocumented — what we call SOPs, standard operating procedures — all the stuff that happened around the software that got ingrained in muscle memory and process, and then external parties.

A CRM may be sticky because a sales rep needs to go in and out of it all the time. They're used to interacting with Salesforce. A lot of times when new VPs of sales come into our companies, they mandate that Salesforce is there because they're used to using it. Their teams are used to using it. And then finance may rely on the Salesforce output for billing, and upstream marketing is going to rely on it as well. There are these dependencies driving stickiness.

The other piece is: you need one single source of truth. You need to know that an account is closed and who is working on it, and all of that needs to be logged in one place. And if you go from CRM to say an ERP or payroll, that absolutely has even legal reasons and compliance reasons why you can't have numbers that are not being tracked as cleanly and correctly as an auditor might like.

So all this drove stickiness and durability, because you were used to using Salesforce, the whole ecosystem was using Salesforce, and it was the default option.

Steven： Yeah. Those are all exactly right. I think it's important to also consider that the most sticky thing you could do is actually collect money from a customer. If you're collecting money, it turns out it's really really hard for them to stop sending you money. And it's really hard for them to figure out what to do if they stop sending you money.

It sounds really trite, but the stickiest software is software that's getting used somewhere. Then when you dig in and try to come up with reasons, well, it just depends on who you talk to in a company. You talk to the HIPAA compliance people in some company and they're going to tell you this is the software you have to use because it's the most bestest HIPAA compliant software. If you talk to the administrators, you're going to hear about onboarding new users. If you talk to the users, you're going to hear about muscle memory and keystrokes or labor unions or whatever.

You really want to get the software sold — and that's your fastest path to sticky. After that it's sort of a winner's tale over what caused it to be sticky. In fact, the best thing about sticky is if you're the rep for a company that you've sold something to and the company is threatening you like "hey we're going to replace you," we're just going to listen to them and you're going to find what's sticky. If that works like three or four times across different accounts, then you've just told the tale as to what made the product sticky. It doesn't matter what the PMs or what anybody else thought of.

Outlook 与 Calendar：一个经典案例

Anyone who's ever tried to displace Microsoft Outlook as email very quickly learned about delegate access and having calendars owned by multiple people and all of this crazy stuff. I can tell you there was no meeting where we said, "Okay, let's figure out how to make the calendar the sticky part of Outlook and make sure we handle recurring meeting exception handling well." And then you find out General Motors isn't going to displace 600,000 seats because of the calendar or some crazy thing like that.

It's really amazing in enterprise software what causes sticky and how you can actually capitalize on it when somebody threatens to take you out of the enterprise.

Sema： I think there's a really good point there in two things. Inertia is a really powerful force. And then the other thing is: nobody, when they're building the software, is thinking about this rubric we put together and going "tick tick tick, we got all these features that are going to do all these things." But the practical reality is: as software extends its tentacles across an organization, and it gets ingrained in people using it, and they've been paying for it for a long time — it just seeps into how people are doing things and that's hard to rip out.

SaaS 末日论？——The Death of Software. Nah.

主持人： You even talk about this in your piece, where there's sort of all of these invisible tacit understandings about how to use different products, or things that are embedded both within the software but also within the people using them. And in fact, Stephen, you've even said the SaaS apocalypse is overblown. You've written an essay called The Death of Software. Nah. where you emphatically rejected this idea. So maybe you want to recount that piece a little bit for us.

Steven： Well, Sema co-wrote a post on SAP, which is sort of the ultimate example of sticky software. The only software that's stickier than SAP is behind the scenes — it's the software that insurance companies wrote. They wrote all this software like 50 years ago or 75 years ago, and there's no replacing it. Whenever jokes come up about businesses that are looking for COBOL programmers, it's to go and work on the insurance software that exists in every state of the union and in many jurisdictions.

One of the biggest successes to date in Stripe has been somebody actually went in and, for the first time in two generations, coded up the software to collect money from people — which itself had previously been an unsolved problem on the scale of insurance, because nobody put together the tax laws for every country, every jurisdiction, every locality, every border crossing, every currency exchange. It's mind-blowing.

So now that is the most sticky. So we'll be doing this podcast with great-grandchildren 100 years from now talking about how sticky that experience was.

编码了外部力量 vs. 编码了公司本身

These examples are ones that codified an external force — and that external force was the regulatory body. The examples of SAP just codified a company. If you take SAP out of a large automobile manufacturer, there's no automobile manufacturer left. Or Walmart — the company just evaporates. Because the company is defined not just by purchasing the software, not even by just using it, but by how they codified the business rules into that product.

Sema： I think it's a good point to double click on, because a misconception right now is that — okay, well, you can just have a Postgres database and APIs and then bam, you can replace SAP. And that's absolutely not true. That piece around the logic and everything else that is encaptured in SAP is way more important than the fact that "oh, this data just happens to be in this database."

There's a reason why SAP takes multiple years to implement. It's not because "oh, the system integrators are slow" — part of it, but it's customized to the way that business actually operates. That's an important part about why you can't just obscure away the software completely and turn it into a database plus APIs.

创业公司的错觉：企业软件真的能被"vibe code"吗？

Steven： This is just so important. Startups look at enterprise software and they think about it in terms of startup scale. So they take something mundane like expense reporting and go: "Well, we have 40 people and one person could figure out expense reports for 40 people. You could hire a human and be done with it." Literally you come back from a trip, you dump the receipts in a bucket and one human rifles through them and the expense reporting problem goes away. Or you say, "Oh, forget the human. We'll just all take pictures of our receipts and OCR it and categorize it and the whole thing will go away."

That's fine — until you have 100,000 people in 20 countries with different national laws and policies about business expense, and then you overlay corporate policies, and then your business is just codified that way and you can't replace it.

Larry Ellison 的"80% 论"

Back in the late 1990s — who is now sort of the godfather of enterprise software — Larry Ellison at Oracle, back then they were only a database company and entering the world of NetSuite and ERP. He went on a multi-year rant about how enterprise software was so stupid because everybody customized it. He had this saying that businesses should just stick with the 80% solution — they should just use whatever works 80% of the time. Most enterprise people were like: (a) You're just talking your book because your software only does 80% of what I need. (b) That's just not how it works.

Take the auto industry — the top 10 companies. Putting aside EV versus gas, they all just make cars, which is a lot of known technology with assembly lines and workers. What differentiates the companies is how they operate and the internal processes to decide what car to make, how much raw material to buy, what currencies to hedge, how many people to hire, when to introduce a new product line. All of that is enterprise resource management and planning. And how is all that done? It's all in SAP.

Those companies are effectively run by people sitting in conference rooms looking at SAP screens. The difference between Ford and Toyota and General Motors and Daimler is not that they're looking at the same screen — it's that they chose which screens to look at, which customizations to make, and then they go buy steel and aluminum and wire and dashboards and radios from all the same places.

I think people wildly underestimate the level of sophistication that customers apply to this software.

Excel 和 Goldman Sachs 的故事

Back when we were first starting to get Excel used in companies — you'll laugh at this — we used to do these little visits, we'd go visit bankers. So we're sitting in Goldman Sachs and we're telling them Excel is better than Lotus 1-2-3.

(That's super old — you don't even know what 1-2-3 is. Trust me, it's old.)

The guy at Goldman looked at us and said, "I don't think you understand. We make more money from Excel than you do."

We're just sitting there like, "What is he talking about?" It made no sense to us. Then we started to think about it: we sell Excel to Morgan Stanley and JP and Chase and everybody else, and what Goldman was saying is their application of Excel is so differentiated. It wasn't just people typing. They built add-ins. They wrote all this code. They defined their workflows.

Sema： There's this wild underestimation about "you could vibe code your way into enterprise software." I was at a dinner last night and there was someone there who was the head of RevOps at maybe a growth-stage startup. His task — this is like a thousand-plus person company — was to rebuild their Salesforce instance internally. He's like, "Oh well, we know all the fields, we can import all the data."

I was like: that's not really the part that's tough. It's — how are you deciding what gets captured? How do the whole organizational lines around it work? And then who's going to maintain this over time? I think that's a piece that just falls off. You can vibe code a CRM. We've all vibe-coded projects that have already gone stale and we haven't touched again because it's painful and it takes time and needs to adapt the business.

建在 SAP 之上：新的生态

主持人： Sema, you've also written about how there's an entire ecosystem of startups now that are just building on top of SAP, that are building around all of the kind of headache-inducing stuff, but still using SAP. So to both of your points earlier, these legacy SaaS systems are just so deeply embedded that the newer insurgents are just coming and building on top and around them, rather than trying to rip out and get people to migrate completely.

Sema： A lot of what we're seeing AI being used for today is: how do you make it — I think the word is often used — conversational? How do you pull the information out and actually make it more usable? How do you retrieve the information from SAP without needing to run a SQL query and get all the information, or look at a bunch of screens?

If I want to be able to connect to Steven's point around analyze — three different sets of tables and different geographies — can I quickly query that in a natural-language way? Can I get reports automatically generated that are customized to me without needing to go back through the SAP customization process?

That usability layer is indicative of what's happening now with software in general: accessing the UI is optional. And going back to the Slackbots point — you want the information delivered to you versus needing to go to the UI. But the data and the business logic inside — either it's SAP or something else replacing it — that all still needs to exist one way or another.

Steven： That's an incredibly important point for folks to take away: the biggest thing about enterprise software is it almost always does what somebody wants it to do — they just don't know how to make it do that. There's no report that SAP can't generate. No graph, no chart, no analysis. But you just can't figure it out. Or maybe it's configured so you don't have permissions.

企业软件的两大隐藏功能：Export to Excel & Export to CSV/PDF

The two most frequently used features exist in no enterprise software natively: Export to Excel and Export as CSV or PDF. All enterprise software — the first thing they have to do when they show up and do that first demo is, the customer asks: "Does it do export to Excel? Does it do export to CSV or PDF?" Because then you know you have an escape valve to do the thing that you couldn't do before on analysis.

What's so cool about where we are today is that now with language models, you have this incredible way to actually consume those much easier than you could before. If you think about PDF — the old way used to be, "Okay, I want to figure out exception handling, some report that my system emits, declined expense reports or whatever, but I want to do it over some weird time period or across different currencies that it doesn't handle." So now you can export them all and take these 20 PDFs and put them in a model and do a bunch of analysis that you couldn't do before.

To something Sema wrote about — these ad hoc business processes are the ones that really become the most interesting, because they're interesting because that's how a business runs, but they're also interesting because those are the next products. Those are the next companies that people start.

CRM used to just be a spreadsheet. If you were in a business and you were an account manager and you kept track of your accounts, you just kept track of it in Excel. And then a company got started to do that. It wasn't SA... it wasn't Salesforce first. It was the predecessor called Siebel. And then people are like, "Oh, we should make a whole company that does this."

That's what some of these apps are that you're seeing — using language models and interfaces that are chat to SAP or to Salesforce. They're just trying to take advantage of what the LLMs are really good at, which is synthesizing and orchestrating unstructured information.

Salesforce 作为"执行机制" + Context 的重要性

Sema： We also forget that Salesforce is really an enforcement mechanism for the go-to-market team. Is the human getting the data to capture the state of the business? (We can talk about Salesforce hygiene, etc., as a separate point.)

Now switch to the agentic world. Imagine there's an agent that needs to do outbound calling or outbound messaging. They want to be able to retrieve that information. They don't really care about how the fields are organized or how many clicks it takes — but they still need to access that information.

Then the second piece they need is context. The internet has talked a lot about context graphs over the last six months. What is that? That's all the exceptions. What do you do? How do you handle certain cases? The edge cases and the permissioning — all the policies that are not necessarily in the fields of Salesforce.

Going back to this 80/20 thing: the agent can extract all the information, send an outbound email based on the persona, but then — how do you deal with one case versus another? "Normally if it's a person in Asia, we respond this way; if it's a person in the US, we respond this other way." That's not captured in Salesforce — that was in someone's head. That's the context that is now really important for agents to be able to act on behalf of this data.

Steven： For Salesforce in particular, that's incredibly important. I've never met a salesperson, an account manager, an account executive who thinks that the default is the right answer for anything with their account. Even if they get the Japanese language right — "oh, it's spring and the birds are chirping and you're overdue on your payment" — the rep is going to want to handle it in their specific way.

This notion of exception handling is just the root of the challenge with agents — almost everything interesting in an enterprise is an exception.

McDonald's 的 Kiosk 类比

Spend 15 minutes at McDonald's and watch people start on the kiosk and give up, and then go watch what they really want. They're like, "Well, I wanted a McFlurry, but I wanted two flavors and mix them together, and that's not in the..." It's always the exceptions. Everything about automation in enterprise is handling exceptions.

Enterprise pricing is a great example: how much is it per seat? Well, you have to call us. Well, you call, then you talk — and then it's still an exception.

Sema： These exceptions aren't captured anywhere right now. Now, if there's a voice agent doing compliance check calls for freight — as one of our portfolio companies does — they're collecting exceptions through their voice agent and getting some of that context. Or if we're looking at computer-using agents — if you're observing humans and how they click through software, how they respond to things — we have this ability now to record interactions and process them with LLMs. Then you're able to start collecting some of this context.

But it's a lot. All the interesting work is around the exceptions. So it's not like, "Okay, three days later, we've got it all, we've got all the context." Also, sales cycles take a long time — each exception isn't handled with the frequency that you get the data immediately. You have to get to that point where "we've observed enough interactions to capture and understand the exceptions." And then on the sales side, the buyer has to trust that this piece of software has captured all the context to handle it.

Amazon 的启示：默认站在客户一边

Steven： Let me add to this because I think it helps us to go back to this notion of headless and what are both the challenges and the opportunities. If you're an engineer, almost everyone talking about what's going on in the world in AI today, you think headless and API and agent API — just interchange them. So you think, "Oh well, it's code, I can write the business process down."

The problem you hit right at the beginning is: if you're not an engineer, you can't even explain the process you use to resolve a customer issue.

It's very interesting to watch Amazon really do some of the best work on this. They really don't want to have humans. You can't call Amazon for anything — it's just hopeless. What they're doing is they're learning with everybody the best way to automate something. It's their religion. It's their core principle: you just decide it in favor of the customer. Oh, they sent the wrong thing? So you go to the chatbot, you tell them. The chatbot understands that you got the wrong thing, and it just sends you a new one.

I think that's so interesting compared to sort of old-school exception handling. And then they use the data to improve the internal shipping and handling and warehouse process. Maybe it's the product description, a zillion other things, or reviews.

That's what I find so interesting about the capabilities of AI: it's driving a different definition and different behavior at companies about how to handle exceptions. When we get through sort of the 1.0 version of this, we're going to get to a new version where people are comfortable letting AI do or decide things, because they realize it's adding a level of predictability and repeatability to their enterprise.

主持人： It's funny to think that maybe customer service gets worse in the short term because things stop getting default-decided in favor of the customer. Suddenly you actually have to defend your case again instead of like being re-shipped the Sensodyne toothpaste you feel like you were owed.

But more generally, it sounds like you're both saying that automating the long tail is still the hardest thing. Is that true, or would you say there are other hard things developers and founders also need to think about?

Permissioning、Systems of Record 和 Agent 之间的交互

Sema： That's part of it. There are a lot of other things around permissioning. You could probably lump it into the long tail, but permissioning is part of this. As you give people or give agents API access, it's like: okay, so in which cases can people extract data? When can they write versus read? That all needs to be figured out over time. And interactions also between agents.

If you go back to the idea of a system of record, ideally one central repository of data that is the source of truth — well, if you have multiple people (and agents) accessing and writing to it, who gets to access when? These are additional problems that need to be solved — solvable, but will take time.

生产力悖论：自动化只会创造更多新工作

Steven： One of the things that happens in technology shifts — everybody knows the thing about nobody understands exponential when it's happening. So you have to be very careful to extrapolate, and you end up extrapolating linear when something exponential is happening.

The same thing happens with productivity: people look at the existing body of work that happens today and say, "Okay, how do we make that easier?" And then all of a sudden there's all this fear that we're going to automate everything away, that everything is just going to become an API, which developers and engineers say, "Oh, that will be easy." And then we'll be in this nirvana world where everything is automated and easy and predictable.

But they forget that productivity drives new scenarios. The minute you can get something easier with automation — and you can actually automate it, which I do think is happening right now with agents and language models — well, then we're going to dream up a whole bunch of new stuff to do.

Amazon 的自我循环

I just mentioned this loop that Amazon must be in on customer service. They got rid of all the phone people and the phone experience that would be miserable — to do a return, and the challenge/response and the fighting. Like, "Can I return this? Do I have to package it up?" Or will you just ignore, like, toothpaste? They don't want it back. That's a pioneering invention by Amazon: if somebody gets the wrong consumable, we just don't want it. Like they're poisoning it, they used part of it, it's cheaper to just have them throw it away.

That never happened before. You used to have to actually bring spoiled food to the supermarket and show it to them.

They've fixed that level of productivity — but now there's this backend that's just out there constantly figuring out how to not have it happen again. Now they need a new level of analysis, a new set of tools. The long tail got no shorter. It just got longer in a different way.

Sema： People forget that that's how innovation works. It's constant reinvention, and it's a growing pie, not a static pie. All the negativity around AI comes from thinking that the work to be done is this fixed thing that takes N people and M amount of software, and we're just going to replace N people with M+5, and then we're done. There's no jobs anymore. There's just an agent running. That's just never going to happen.

Legal is a great example — where people do contracts and they think that AI is going to help contracts get done quicker without lawyers. Except I can assure you contracts will get longer and more sophisticated and encompass way more sets of scenarios than a person ever could. That's going to create a whole set of more litigation around it, and that creates a whole ecosystem.

放射科的例子

Look, there's the now apocryphal, semi-apocalyptical famous example of radiology — which is a correlation, not a causation — but radiologists all love AI, and now we are having a radiology shortage. It's not just... there's a lot of reasons. It's complicated. But it shows that the innovation wasn't static and the market for demand wasn't static.

差旅费的进化

A lot of what happens at the enterprise level is: the minute you automate the most mundane thing and think you have it all squared away, whole new things appear. Expense reporting is a really good example. First there's nothing. Then people figure out how to do spreadsheets. Then people figure out — oh, now we have a whole system, we can analyze it. Now business travel, you get ahead of the curve. "Well, let's use miles for business travel. Let's route our travel requests to the best prices we could get at any given moment rather than just default to one carrier. Let's use a specific credit card for business travel that buys us a bunch of different added benefits that we know matter to our patterns of travel."

Suddenly there's a bigger job called business travel analysis that takes way more people than just booking the flights — which everybody can just do on their own.

There's always another layer of analysis on top. Always there. But the analysis then drives new processes and new behaviors that themselves differentiate companies.

Sema： The other thing interesting — not to spend too much time on business travel — there are the physical and digital worlds. There will always be things that humans are doing. Maybe it's not back-office TPS reports, but salespeople will be closing deals. There will be human interaction to close deals. People will be getting on planes as a result. Maybe they aren't spending as much time entering data into Salesforce, but there will be these humans doing online and offline work. There will always be a data exhaust from things to capture optimization that needs to happen. And that isn't going away either.

开源软件、"完成"的艺术，与决定的本质

Steven： Open-source software development is actually a really good example of this. The hardest thing in software development is: you have to be finished at some point, so that everybody knows this is a stable release and can go build on it. The art of finishing is this long tail of not changing the code. And there's no API for that.

Developers don't hesitate to think there should be no API for that. They could think of a way to automate it with voting and with a discussion that has sentiment analysis or whatever — but you still need a bunch of people to concur over a decision to fix or not fix something.

Yet they'll advocate that some other business process, like closing the books for earnings, should just be an API. It's actually literally the same mental model: there's a bunch of stuff and we're deciding when to close the books and what sales to account for. It's fixing a bug — and there's a story around it, a narrative — and we have to explain it to our boss, and if something goes wrong, we need a trail that explains who did what.

So much of what a business really is, is just the people deciding things. All that software does is uplevel, abstract, and change what they decide, and how, and what tools they use.

Sema： The other piece is: it's the best case for just recording everything you do. If people are going and flying and closing deals in person, make sure the software or the LLM can capture everything that happens at all times. Obviously not advocating for full panopticon, but —

Steven： — synthetic gathering.

Sema： Exactly. Whether it's recording conversations or taking emails and written artifacts and ingesting them — that is the way that the world is moving.

Steven： It's also — to your earlier point — expertise exists in this cloud in an organization, and it is the untapped resource of the modern era. Aaron Levie at Box has done the most eloquent job of explaining repeatedly the assets that exist in all of these Word and Excel documents strewn throughout a company. It's actually very hard to understand which documents are important, which ones to believe. Part of being in a company and having a culture is really knowing the answer to that. It's super interesting to watch the customers at Box use Box to answer those questions: which are the sales PowerPoint presentations that are actually working? Which are the spreadsheets and the models that people actually rely on?

I think AI is the first thing to come along that really taps into that unstructured information in a company.

历史的韵脚：MCP、中间件与微软的反垄断案

主持人： Before we wrap up, it might be good to visit the more immediate history and then the more faraway history of what headless software even is. Stephen, you wrote last year a piece in reaction to the rise of MCP servers. In that piece you also related it to early Microsoft litigation that the Justice Department levied against them — part of the argument was that Microsoft had a lot of products that could be categorized as middleware. In all of these different software waves that you've witnessed, in what ways is history rhyming and repeating? Maybe not on the litigation side, but on the product level.

Steven： That part will continue too, yes. It's super interesting. And I love Sema opining on where she sees things going with startups in this regard.

But the real thing with MCP is — everything we're seeing now is that so much of it is driven by an engineering view of what would make for a good software architecture, and very little of it is being driven by seamlessly the physical reality of the world. Of course if you're an engineer, you'd love every tool you want to use to have a very clean API, preferably like a command-line interface that pipes text in and out — that would be perfect.

But that turns out to not be how the world wants to work. There are many reasons why it doesn't want to work that way. Sema touched on many — security, compliance, and things like that. But the reality is that no software wants to be disintermediated by some other layer above it.

Nobody wants to just be put in a corner and told: "Your job is to just store the SQL format for expense reports and do nothing more, and then we're going to use you only for that, and by the way, we're piping you through to some other tool to analyze expense reports." Because that's not a growing business — that's a decaying business.

This whole notion that everybody is going to be perfectly content to be abstracted by some benign layer in the middle — it just doesn't really work that way. Customers actually do not want to assemble their scenario from a bunch of different providers, because all it takes is: your system will only be as stable as the most unstable part of that. If the expense report company goes out of business, you're completely out of luck. So you want your expense report company to be thriving and doing more stuff — even though in your head you're like, "I wish they would just stop. I don't want any more from them. It's getting complicated. Oh, they just did a UI reworking that's driving me crazy."

And the flip side is: those companies, they're not just going to sit there and decay. They're going to look to the left and look to the right, and they're going to do the stuff that they see people using with their product. SAP — the example Sema used — we're seeing this whole ecosystem grow up, and SAP is just going to do those things. Not all of them, and most of them they're not going to do very well. In fact, just before this I was talking to somebody and we reminded them that in most giant enterprise companies, they view just a tie with some competitor as a win — because they'll just bundle it into their existing thing and give it away.

But this middleware layer is always very unstable. It looks great in a network hierarchy diagram of the OSI levels of networking, but it's just never that stable.

三种路径：面对新一代 AI 软件，企业该怎么选？

Sema： Two things I'll add.

One is the practical realities. Go back to the Salesforce example — or like Workday. Workday has had APIs that you could work with. But can you really extract all of the data out of Workday in a clean way and just operate without using Workday? No. Workday makes it extremely difficult to actually get access to the documentation and work with it. They don't expose all the endpoints. The API example is analogous to what we're seeing now: it makes it a dumb database — and they're not incentivized to do that.

So there's three paths in front of you as a consumer or business looking to buy software:

路径一：把 Salesforce 变成后端

Take Salesforce and either turn on Agentforce or build all my agents on top of it, and then treat Salesforce as kind of just the backend. Some of that will work, but some of that will also not work — because Salesforce doesn't want to be just the data in the background. So I think there will be mixed results around that. I'm not bullish on the incumbent software building great agents on top.

路径二：完全 DIY

You just totally DIY it. You have the most control in that situation. However, that's really hard. You have to rebuild true enterprise software. For a startup, rebuilding a CRM — much easier. Rebuilding a CRM for a Fortune 500 business — it's a lot of business logic to capture. And you're also trying to do open-heart surgery while the patient is alive.

Steven： Well, hopefully they're alive, but —

Sema： Yeah. Yes. Of course they're alive. But I mean, you're like taking the engine out mid-flight, whatever analogy. That's really hard, and you have to get the practical realities of permissioning and collaboration and all that right.

路径三：新一代 AI 软件在旁边生长

There's a third option — and this is why we continue to do what we do in investing in AI software. There is a reason that agents can continue to be built, the data can be sucked in and built in the background. A lot of what we're seeing right now is things that are working alongside SAP or a layer of visibility on top that is enhancing the experience and allowing the business user to run agents on top of the existing data they have — but also not throw out all of the logic they've had in the background.

Also, create a new system of record. Voice agents are collecting new data, recordings are collecting new data, transcription and ingestion of documents — all of that is pulling in. Maybe one day these AI startups will replace the systems of record in the back end. But they are doing so in a systematic way of observing how the business is operating.

创业公司最大的机会在哪里？

主持人： I guess to close this out, Sema, where are we really seeing the biggest opportunities for startups right now?

Sema： A lot of this — yeah, it's doing the things that the incumbents are not doing right now. Going from a layer of collection of data into how do we take action on top of it.

Take the CRM example: I'm not just logging all of the call information, but now I'm providing the intelligence back — how do I prioritize leads, which accounts should we work on, what has risk of churn, flagging all of that, and then sending the outbound.

Part of that is creating this agentic loop: - As the agent sends the outbound, sees the response, you're understanding: what works, what didn't, how did people respond? - You're also collecting benchmark data: "this type of response is most effective in these cases; in Asia we should be using this type of opening; in Europe etc."

You're now agentically collecting all of that — that's an interesting data exhaust. So I think that's another area.

The third area I'd flag: we talked about physical realities. A lot of the vertical software that builds for the physical world is a really interesting set of data that has been hard to capture historically. Agents have been able to operate on software, but then also what humans are doing out in the field, machines out in the field — pulling that back in. Construction, manufacturing, all of that.

Steven 的策略建议：不要正面进攻，瞄准夹缝

Steven： Well, the universal truth for enterprise software is: the most difficult thing to do that happens to be the dumbest is to attempt to just compete head-on with an existing category. By head-on, I mean not just the same category, but doing it the same way.

The biggest opportunity right now is always to look at the existing mental map of enterprise categories and be in between two established players — because the thing you know right now during a massive technology shift is: the one thing established players won't do is disturb their existing product line and go-to-market. They absolutely will just be bolting AI on top of their existing product. They won't be getting rid of it. They won't stop working on it. They won't do anything to break it. They're just going to try to weather this technology storm by powering through it.

So your opportunity in a startup is to look at two big players who are bolting AI onto the side and exposing some existing API as an agent — and just aim for the middle, and do things in the new way.

By not attacking head-on, you don't show up at every single customer and have them go, "Well, you need to do these 8,000 things before you even enter the door." Instead, you have an equally difficult question, but one you're in control of: why do you even exist? That's your own question. You don't have to answer to a 20-year-old framework that got created to answer a bunch of questions that aren't even relevant anymore.

HTTP 和 HTML 的历史类比

The best example of this is HTTP and HTML. Client-server existed, but the reason that those took over was not because it did all the things that client-server did. In fact, it did none of them. But it implemented that concept in an entirely new way. So the web exists in spite of the fact that legacy vendors had a trillion dollars invested on how client-servers should work.

Sema： The other piece too: it's not just between two legacy vendors, but there's also a layer of translation between two different functions within an organization.

Steven： Oh yeah. Yeah, for sure.

Sema： Software has always sold to "I'm selling into just the sales team or the finance team," but then there's these handoffs — which is now the context, right? — but on bills and deals and like that. That actually also presents an interesting opportunity.

最后一问：企业软件会有网络效应吗？

Sema： The last question I have, which is for Stephen. Network effects is this thing we always talk about on the consumer side, and it's a great source of defensibility. No enterprise software business, as far as I can tell, has successfully implemented network effects. But you could argue that is a good source of durability over time. Salesforce has tried this in a couple of ways in the past. Do you think enterprise software will start entering the field of network effects — in terms of, "okay, we're going to have both buyers and sellers on our CRM and therefore mediate these transactions"? I'm curious to get your take on that.

Steven： Certainly network effects outside of a company are extremely difficult, for a bunch of compliance and security reasons. But the biggest network effect in enterprise software is inside of a company. And we're seeing that happen now with just chat.

All of a sudden you're seeing — it's so incredible to be at this dynamic that almost felt like the good old days when some very motivated person... Most people who work in enterprises, it turns out, are not super interested in making their job better. They actually just want to go to work, get paid, and go home. They don't come to work every day going, "How can I streamline my task?" They just want to not mess it up. That is a lot of the world.

But there's a small set of people — like those bankers at Goldman Sachs — that were like, "How do I do more deals faster, better, more clever models?" So they were using Excel when the other bankers were using 1-2-3.

1980 年代 Excel 广告和 2025 年 ChatGPT 时刻

Floating around on the internet is this old commercial for Excel, the launch TV ad from the late 1980s where the first Excel spreadsheets were being used. It's a person sitting there with this monstrous laptop that weighed like 12 pounds, in an elevator, trying to use it. I'm laughing because of course they were trying to not run out of battery life in the elevator ride, which was invariably the case. But all of a sudden this crowded elevator of a bunch of people in these 1980s ties and 1980s wearing glasses looking at the spreadsheet going, "What are you doing? How are you doing that?" — and getting all excited.

Fast forward to 2025, and that's exactly what happened with chat. I had a friend at SAP that was writing a white paper about something, and I just asked them, "Tell me what questions you're trying to answer." I did the prompt and sent them back a white paper. And I'm positive I kicked off some sort of viral loop — not technically a viral loop, but some sort of network-effect viral loop inside of her team, because all of a sudden people are seeing how to make their job better, and it's accessible to them, and they're doing it.

So — to your point, Tim — this idea of a tool that enables two functions to talk together that couldn't before is golden. That's literally what enterprise software integration is, except that's all manual brute-force "hire Accenture" kind of stuff.

If you have products that bridge this — Figma did a bunch of this with design and product development. If you can develop software that leverages AI in order to bring together parts of an organization that don't normally communicate, that's a new category. We've seen that with things like IT budgeting, where IT and finance would end up with tools that helped them both do forecasting, and the cloud enabled that. That's a huge opportunity.

结束语

主持人： Nice. Well, I think that's also an amazing note to end on. Thank you so much, Stephen, for joining us here. Thanks, Sema.

Sema： Yeah. And thank you.

Steven： And thank you, Sema.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：Agent 不是软件的杀手，而是软件的"哈勃望远镜"
大多数人在讨论"headless software"时，都把目光聚焦在**UI 死了**这件事上。但视频中真正令人震撼的洞察藏在 Steven 一句几乎被忽略的话里——他说"agent 其实就是一个'跑很久但可能永远跑不完的程序'，我们过去把这叫 bug，现在叫 feature"。
这句话像一把撬棍，撬开了整个 SaaS 讨论的地基：**agent 的真正革命性不在于"替代人"，而在于把企业内部第一次可以承受"长时间、非确定性、可反复迭代"的计算过程**。这意味着我们第一次能"看见"那些原本因为算力太贵、注意力太稀缺而被压抑的业务问题——就像哈勃望远镜第一次让人类看见深空一样。
所以"software losing its head"这个命题反过来才成立：**软件不是失去了头，而是第一次长出了会做梦的大脑**。UI 的消失是表象，真正在发生的是"企业认知深度"的解锁。
## 跨领域连接：企业软件的"暗物质"与人类学中的"隐性知识"
Sema 反复提到的"context graph"、"excepti

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Tennis Executive Aiming To Transform One of The Most Political Environments In Sport
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被误读的"赛事时间轴"：真正的权力时刻藏在资格赛周

最反直觉的信号出现在他对四大满贯节奏的拆解——"资格赛周才是最激烈的一周"，而不是决赛周。当外界（包括媒体、粉丝、赞助商传播）把注意力聚焦在最后的冠军点时，行业内部的真实"权力密度"恰恰发生在球迷还没到场的那几天：经纪人到齐、球员到齐、合作伙伴到齐，所有交易、续约、争议、政治博弈都在这个"前台尚未开演"的窗口完成。

这揭示了一个体育产业的双层结构：B2C 的高潮（决赛）与 B2B 的高潮（资格赛周）在时间上是错位的，甚至几乎不重叠。用金融市场类比，这就像散户盯着收盘价，而机构早已在盘前完成了所有关键仓位调整。真正决定一年产业格局的会谈，发生在观众以为"还没开始"的时候。

二、"四个商业峰会"框架：Grand Slam 的隐藏定义

他一句话就重写了大满贯的定义——"四大满贯就像是一年四次大型商业会议和工作坊"。这不是随口一说，而是一个内部人士的操作系统。一旦你用这个框架看整个巡回赛，就会发现：

  • ATP/WTA 巡回赛其实是季度性 offsite 的连接组织；
  • 球员是产品，也是与会代表；
  • 教练、经纪、赞助商构成典型的 conference ecosystem；
  • 决赛的意义反而更像是"大会闭幕式"——象征意义大于交易意义。

这与硅谷的 Allen & Company Sun Valley 峰会、达沃斯论坛的结构惊人相似：真正的价值不在讲台，而在走廊。网球高管的心智模型，其实更接近投行 MD，而不是俱乐部经理。

三、"不回答问题"的深层逻辑：他其实在做政治家训练，而非公关训练

他半开玩笑地引用 Andy Roddick 的话："处理媒体的方式，就是不回答被问的问题"，然后立刻补一句 COVID 让他学到的媒体技能超过职业生涯任何时期。这里有一个被大多数观众漏掉的信号——

他在描述的其实不是网球高管的公关技巧，而是危机时期政治领导人的信息控制术：设定议题、锚定叙事、用透明感换取议程权。跨领域看，这套方法论与新西兰 Ardern、新加坡李显龙在疫情初期的沟通策略高度同构：用可控的坦诚，交换议题的主导权。

于是一个隐藏的系统性真相浮现——顶级体育赛事 CEO 越来越像一国之首：他们管理的不是比赛，而是"利益相关方的情绪曲线"（球员工会、公共卫生部门、地方政府、赞助商、直播平台、球迷协会）。当他说"网球是运动中最政治化的环境之一"时，他真正在暗示的是：未来体育行业的稀缺人才不是营销天才，而是能同时读懂选票、财报和更衣室情绪的"体育政治家"。这也是为什么很多顶级赛事管理者的下一站，往往不是另一项运动，而是政府或跨国治理机构。

📝 转录文本

Opening: Welcome & Setting

Craig, hello. Welcome to the Business of Sport. It's amazing to have you in the studio in London.

It's great. It's good to be here. I wouldn't expect to be in London having a chat to you, but timing of the year works out well with the Wimbledon Championships happening at the same time.

Yeah, we're recording this on day one, so I'd imagine a lot of the, well, the work may not have started for you yet, or have you been here for a few days?

Qualifying Week: The Real Intensity

No, we've been, we've been pretty involved. Qualifying week is always an intense week for us, not only supporting our athletes that are playing, but also starting to get into all the things that we need to do — and then this week as well is pretty intense.

It's the business side of the event really, at the end of qualifying in the first week. It's where all the agents are there, all the players are there, the partners that they have — and it's where the most activity actually happens.

And then of course when you get to the final week of a Grand Slam, that's when it's more about the tennis, and the tennis purists tend to be more focused on that final week.

Do You Enjoy It?

Do you guys enjoy it?

Yeah, love it. I mean, I don't get to watch as much as you'd like to, but if you get a moment to watch a little bit you do — but for the most part you're out there working and being around it.

It's kind of like the four Grand Slams are like having four major business conferences and workshops a year, and that's how we approach it.

On Media, Andy Roddick & Transparency

So when I was taking a look before this interview, I always like to see what people have done beforehand — and you're a seasoned pro in front of the microphone. So much so that I actually watched something you did with Andy Roddick, and to quote you, you said one thing you learned from him: "that's how you deal with the media, you just don't answer the questions you're asked." So I thought, well this is going to be brilliant. We're going to have a lot of fun in here.

I've been coached to always say what you want to say regardless of what you're asked. I've been on a journey through COVID, which was a very intense period for us, and I think I learned media management skills probably more than any other time in my career.

But I've been fortunate to be on a journey where you deal with the media a lot and conversation a lot, and I tend to always say what I think. And sometimes that doesn't go down well, but people at least appreciate the transparency.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被误读的"赛事时间轴"：真正的权力时刻藏在资格赛周
最反直觉的信号出现在他对四大满贯节奏的拆解——**"资格赛周才是最激烈的一周"，而不是决赛周**。当外界（包括媒体、粉丝、赞助商传播）把注意力聚焦在最后的冠军点时，行业内部的真实"权力密度"恰恰发生在球迷还没到场的那几天：经纪人到齐、球员到齐、合作伙伴到齐，所有交易、续约、争议、政治博弈都在这个"前台尚未开演"的窗口完成。
这揭示了一个体育产业的**双层结构**：B2C 的高潮（决赛）与 B2B 的高潮（资格赛周）在时间上是错位的，甚至几乎不重叠。用金融市场类比，这就像散户盯着收盘价，而机构早已在盘前完成了所有关键仓位调整。真正决定一年产业格局的会谈，发生在观众以为"还没开始"的时候。
## 二、"四个商业峰会"框架：Grand Slam 的隐藏定义
