item.description:
## 一、"十亿美元 PDF" 其实是文明级的默认操作系统
表面上看，这集节目讨论的是"叙事在资本市场的定价能力"。但真正惊人的是这个隐喻的**颗粒度**：不是"故事"、不是"愿景"，而是一个**PDF**——一份可以被复制、粘贴、在群聊里转发、被算法切成 clips 的文件。
这暗示了一个被反复忽视的事实：**在信息稀缺时代，叙事是"发现"的；在信息过载时代，叙事是"配发"的。** PDF 之所以值十亿美金，不是因为它对，而是因为它**可以被携带**。这与中世纪的《圣经》抄本、清代的科举范文、冷战期间兰德的政策白皮书是同一个物种——一种在恐慌期为群体提供"共同焦点"的**便携式秩序装置**。
**意外的观察**：真正的"十亿美元 PDF"从来不是最深刻的那份，而是**恰好足够聪明、又足够容易被误读的那一份**。因为完全正确的东西无法在传播中变形，而变形才是资本得以"跟随足球"的物理条件。你要做的不是写对，而是留出被算法二次加工的空间。
---
## 二、"贵族制的回归" 藏在 Cap Table 和 SPV 里
嘉宾提到了一个惊人的观察：SpaceX / Anthropi

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Inside Zipline's Autonomous System: 140M Miles, Zero Incidents
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"15%悖论"：被低估的隐形基础设施

Keller反复强调：飞机只占整个解决方案复杂度的15%。这是本次对话中最反直觉的商业洞察——它颠覆了几乎所有硬件叙事。

真正震撼的是：Zipline在早期把飞机当作99%的答案，结果签了21家医院却只服务了1家，工程师在Rwanda凌晨集体崩溃。这个"15%教训"其实是所有硬件公司都要付的学费——从Tesla Roadster用Lotus底盘失败，到SpaceX不得不自建每一颗螺丝。硬件产品的可见部分永远只是冰山，真正决定成败的是看不见的85%：库存管理、民航整合、需求预测、维修网络……

这里有一个隐藏的、几乎没人指出的连接：这15%/85%的比例，与LLM时代惊人一致。今天所有做AI应用的公司都在重复Zipline的错误——以为模型就是产品。GPT-4只是那15%，剩下85%是评估管道、上下文工程、身份认证、失败恢复、监管应对。Zipline在没有大模型的十年里，替整个AI应用行业提前跑通了这道题。

二、"病人不分昼夜"：产品市场契合度的黑暗版本

Eric讲的Rwanda故事表面上是"客户中心主义"，但仔细听会发现一个极其反常识的PMF信号：

"我们期待医生给出关于无人机的反馈，结果他们说：人是24/7生病的，你们凭什么只开12小时？"

大多数创业者衡量PMF的方式是"用户是否喜欢"。但这里给出了一个更黑暗、更精准的标准——用户是否在生气地索要更多。

这与传统SaaS世界的NPS哲学是完全相反的：NPS问"你会推荐吗"，本质是询问礼貌的、有余裕的用户；而Zipline遇到的是"你不给我就有人会死"的用户。这是产品市场契合度的极端形态——不是喜爱，是依赖到愤怒。

一个跨领域连接：这种"愤怒的PMF"与心理学中的"贝尔纳斯基愤怒曲线"（对基础设施失灵的愤怒程度反映其被内化的程度）是同构的。你只会对水电煤断供发火，不会对Netflix宕机发火。Zipline在建立配送时，就同步在肯尼亚和卢旺达人心里建立了"这是基础设施"的心理契约——这才是它无法被复制的护城河。

三、隐藏在"零事故"背后的算术恐怖

Keller给出了一个让人后背发凉的换算：

"如果你开车走1亿4千万英里，会有600起事故、100人受伤、2到6人死亡。"

我们所有人都活在这个统计学的雾中而不自知。汽车的死亡率高到，我们只能通过"零事故的机器人"来看清它的真面目。 这里有一个非常特别的哲学时刻：自动化的真正贡献，不在于它比人做得好，而在于它像一面镜子，第一次量化了人类操作的默认地狱。

这个洞察可以延伸到一个更宏大的跨领域观察：Zipline的"从Whimo的2倍安全"目标，是空运版的摩尔定律。 Alfred在董事会上说"跟车比是BS，跟Whimo比"——这不是一句玩笑，这是基准的重置。当Waymo成为新的基线，所有物流、载人、载物系统的安全预期都会被拉高一个数量级。人类会开始拒绝坐飞机，因为"飞机安全性还不如Zipline"。这是一个即将到来的安全通胀。

四、意想不到的观察：养老院是无人机的秘密引擎

对话中一个只讲了30秒但极具颠覆性的信号：养老院是Zipline的高需求节点。80岁的祖母一年下单350次。

这打破了硅谷根深蒂固的偏见——"技术是给年轻人的"。真相是：技术的最大受益者永远是行动力最弱的人。 iPhone和Face ID让下单变得比走到街角更容易，于是移动能力最受限的群体成为最忠实的用户。

这里的跨领域连接极其惊人：这与19世纪末电话最先在偏远地区普及是同一个模式（Fischer的《America Calling》研究过）；也与Uber在残疾人社区渗透率极高是同构的。技术从来不是"极客的玩具"——它是行动约束者的解放工具。Zipline正在无意识地成为一个抗衰老基础设施，而没人在讨论这件事。

五、埋在数据里的"5.5亿变55亿"炸弹

最后一个几乎被顺便提及的、但可能是整场对话最重要的数据点：

"如果把Dallas观察到的购买行为扩展到全美，即时配送市场不是55亿次/年，而是550亿次/年。"

Uber早期投资人测算错了taxi市场的规模——Uber最终是taxi市场的10倍。因为当你把摩擦降到近零，消费行为会质变。

这个洞察连接到一个更深的规律：基础设施的存在本身会创造它自己所服务的需求。 高速公路建成后，人们不是"更方便地"出行了——他们变成了不同的人（郊区居民、通勤者、周末旅行者）。Zipline建成后，5分钟送达不会让你"更方便地"买咖啡——它会让你取消家里的咖啡机、不再一次性大采购、改变冰箱的容量选择。

Zipline不是在配送商品，它在配送"未来的自我"。 这个自我不需要提前规划、不需要囤积、不需要拥有——只需要按需召唤。而这正是Keller说的"给地球一份还给人类"的真正含义：不只是物理空间的解放，更是心理囤积欲的解放。

📝 转录文本

开场：卢旺达医生的一句反馈

I remember being in Rwanda early days and going out and meeting with some of the doctors and lab techs that we were serving and asking for them like, you know, how's it going? What what do you think? What's your feedback? Here I am kind of uh, you know, upand-coming, you know, learning engineer thinking they're going to say something about the drone or some of these things. And the main piece of feedback that I received was: people get sick 24/7, why are you guys only open 12 hours a day?

Right — especially when you're delivering life-saving blood.

Yeah exactly. And so that was a really key insight for me where it's like, man, we've found product market fit in a market where — yeah, our product wasn't great yet, but it was solving a real need. Having that that really beachhead market where there's a real problem being solved, and when your customer is telling you that their main feedback is they want more of your service, it's like that's a good sign.

欢迎与背景介绍

Welcome Keller and Eric to the show. You guys have been working at Zipline for a long period of time. Keller is the co-founder and Eric, you are in charge of systems engineering and safety. We got lots of things to talk about in this whole world of drones, drone systems, and how you guys started in this hardware space before LLMs even started. So we have lots of questions.

Thanks for having us.

Zipline 不是一家"无人机公司"

But you don't like Zipline being described as a drone company, even though you're probably the largest autonomous drone company in the world right now.

I mean, we've always wanted to be an extremely customer-obsessed company. And the reality is none of our customers care at all about drones. Our goal was always to build an automated logistic system for Earth and to approximate teleportation. And all the customers who are living on Zipline today, they really don't care about the technology operating behind the curtain. What they care about is their ability to download an app, open it up, see a huge number of different brands and amazing restaurants that they want to shop with, and then click a button and have it delivered to them five minutes later. So we've always really tried to focus on the experience rather than on the specific technology.

Well, this show is about technology. We're excited about that. This podcast is about technology. What is the underlying technology behind Zipline?

逆风起航：在 AI 时代之前做机器人

You started in 2011. You pivoted in 2014. This is way before anything related to AI, robotics, foundation models, anything related to that. You were before all of that, and you're riding the wave of all of the things that have come after as well.

Yeah, this was when starting a robotics company was the dumbest thing you could possibly do. It wasn't easy, and it was particularly hard because so many of those conversations — I mean, I was 23, 24, Eric joined the company around that time, and we were starting to describe this vision of an autonomous logistics system for Earth that would be 10 times as fast, half the cost, zero emission. One of the biggest problems when we were trying to raise money for that vision was investors would say, "Isn't this illegal in the US?" In fact, I think that's a question you asked me when we started talking about this.

We weren't allowed to fly beyond visual line of sight.

I mean, we weren't allowed to fly at all really. And so the answer was yes, it's illegal. And then most investors would be like, "Well, we don't invest in illegal things, so we're not going to invest." But for weird reasons this is what basically took Zipline down this path of: well, if it's illegal in the US, then we can launch in other parts of the world where the value of the service would be extremely high.

卢旺达：从血液配送开始

Zipline decided to launch in Rwanda in 2016 delivering blood transfusions directly to hospitals and primary care facilities. This enabled us to have a use case that was so powerful that a government would work very closely with us to make it happen.

To make it legal — or at least make an exemption to their existing regulatory framework.

When we launched in 2016, we were like: "We have this really cool drone, we put all this work into designing these really cool aircraft." And when we launched it was a total disaster. In that first year — for the first eight months — we had signed a contract to serve 21 hospitals, and we served one hospital for the first nine months.

Eric in particular — how much did you sleep during those...?

Spent a lot of time in Rwanda. Didn't sleep a lot.

And then when you're in the US, we'd get woken up at like midnight because that's when the distribution center was turning on and everything would be broken, nothing was working. It was totally desperate — constant all-nighters and working through the weekends — because we made this big error, which was thinking that the cool vehicle was the majority of the solution.

"15% 定律"：飞机只占复杂度的一小部分

What we learned during that first year is that the drone is 15% of the complexity of the solution.

The physical drone, the hardware of it, is only 15%?

We had to build so many auxiliary software systems, maintenance systems — how do we hold the inventory and do inventory management? How do we integrate with a national civil aviation authority? How do we integrate with a national health care system? How do we do ordering and demand management? We had to build out all of these other parts of the overall logistics system.

So this is the reason a lot of people might look at Zipline and be like, "Wow, it's a cool drone company." The reality is the aircraft is 15% of the solution that's required to build something that just feels like magical reliable teleportation 24/7, 365, to the now hundreds of millions of people who depend on the service.

"24/7 是不够的"：客户教给我们的第一课

Speaking of 24/7, I remember being in Rwanda early days and going out and meeting with some of the doctors and lab techs that we were serving. The main piece of feedback that I received was: people get sick 24/7. Why are you guys only open 12 hours a day?

Right, especially when you're delivering life-saving blood.

Yeah, exactly. You got to start somewhere, right? So we started being open 12 hours a day and trying to expand and grow from there. That was a really key insight for me — we'd found product market fit in a market where, yeah, our product wasn't great yet, but it was solving a real need.

We were 24/7 within the first year. We're now 24/7, 365. On Christmas day I usually call all of our different distribution centers to thank them and check in with them. There is no day when these facilities don't depend on us. We went from serving one to 20 to 500, now to 5,000 hospitals and health facilities across the world, across eight countries. It's become the largest commercial autonomous system on Earth.

规模：1.4 亿英里、零事故

Eric — can you size that for us? The largest system on Earth?

Just crossed 140 million commercial autonomous miles.

I mean, how many times is that? Sun and back?

One of the things that I like is: every road in the United States — driving on every single road more than 30 times.

Wow. That's a good stat.

Seeing the impact that that system is now having across all these eight countries — University of Pennsylvania just published a study showing a 51% reduction in maternal mortality thanks to Zipline. Half as many moms losing their lives in childbirth. Across all the different use cases Zipline serves, some of our partners estimate that we're saving between 10 and 12,000 lives a year. And that impact is growing exponentially as we're now expanding, especially as a result of this new partnership we have with the US State Department.

与美国国务院的 5.5 亿美元合作

What is that partnership with the State Department?

In December, we announced a $550 million partnership with the US State Department to expand the impact of Zipline's life-saving service across a lot of the countries where we're already operating. With USAID being shut down, the US was really seeking new ways of engaging in these countries and helping save lives, but they wanted to do it in a way that would accelerate the economies of these countries and help the US economically.

The new strategy they're calling "commercial diplomacy" — the idea is that all of the developing world should be built on top of US AI and robotics technology. When you talk to these countries about what they want, they'll tell you they are sick of low-quality aid provided by NGOs for free, because these services engender dependence and prevent economic growth. What they want is high-paying jobs, entrepreneurship, technology. So the US is going through a big strategic shift: let's basically incentivize these countries to adopt that kind of infrastructure, make sure that as these countries are accelerating they're doing it using US robotics and AI technology.

十年生产运行学到的教训

I'm curious about — you guys now run the largest autonomous system in the world, and you launched it 10 years ago. You've been in production for 10 years. You've learned a lot of stuff that your average engineer sitting behind a computer screen has no idea they're going to run into when they try to deploy AI into the real world. What popped up over the last 10 years that you never would have guessed you needed to be good at?

We started off delivering life-saving products. Our customers need life-saving products all the time in all weather conditions. You would think it's wind, these things, but one of the weirdest things is actually solar weather. There are solar flares that happen on the sun — big explosions that send radiation to the earth. They can mess with the ionosphere, and that can cause RF signals coming from GPS satellites to be faster or slower than you expect. That can lead to degradation and challenges in navigation systems.

So we designed our navigation system and our GNSS systems to be robust to these conditions, to ensure that we can still know where aircraft are with centimeter-level precision in those challenging solar flare times, as well as designing the system to have redundancy beyond GNSS such that if things get really bad we can still safely operate.

Compute Failover：假设任何部件都可能出故障

Eric, you're in charge of safety. Tell us about what you've learned about safety today, and specifically about the compute failover system that you have.

One of the things that we're proud of is how we've developed compute failover. There's a flight computer that flies the aircraft. Lots of sensors come into this computer, and it does a lot of math and sends commands to actuators — motors, control surfaces. This is the brain that flies the aircraft.

One of the things we've learned is: you need to assume that any part of the system can have a fault. Could be a software challenge, a connector challenge —

Bit flip due to solar radiation —

All kinds of things. So what we've done is we have two flight computers, and both of these flight computers think that they're flying the aircraft at any given point in time. They're all receiving all the information from the sensors, they're all sending commands to the actuators, and there's a kind of a third "orbiter" — a little computer that is monitoring the health of those two and telling every other node on the aircraft who to listen to, who's actually in charge.

What if the orbiter fails?

If the arbiter fails, then the primary computer that was flying just keeps flying. If the thing that's monitoring its health fails, then now we say okay, we're just going to keep flying on the thing that was good and we're going to keep flying the mission.

Two heads are better than one. We actually had one of these events happen a couple weeks ago. After a delivery, we delivered the package to the customer and then we had a hiccup on the main flight computer, and we switched over to the backup. Aircraft flew itself home, landed — everything was totally fine. Designing the systems to be robust and reliable through and through is how you get to two and a half million deliveries and 140 million miles flown with no safety incidents.

用手机供应链造航空级冗余

And a lot of what Zipline's doing — it's not like "oh, this is totally revolutionary, no one has ever thought about having a secondary flight computer." That's how a Boeing 777 works. But the cost of a flight computer on a Boeing 777 is in the millions of dollars. A lot of what Zipline's having to do is take a lot of the best ideas that you can see in aerospace safety best practices, and then you got to figure out how to build that using components coming out of the smartphone supply chain.

So you can do it for tens of dollars or hundreds of dollars. You can achieve similar levels of safety to traditional aerospace, but you can move 100 times as fast at 1/100th of the cost.

那另外 85% 是什么？测试与验证的深度

So you mentioned that the aircraft is only 15%. Describe the other 85% in layers.

How do we test? Why do we test? We're not a software company — we're a real-world AI robotics company. There are electromechanical systems out in the real world. So there's hardware test aspects, software test aspects, and integrated system test aspects.

On the hardware side, we do a lot of component-level testing, HALT testing — highly accelerated lifetime testing — where we're taking components, maybe it's a motor, and we're putting them through hell. Making it rain, making it hot, making it humid, making it corrosive, while we're spinning the motor, moving things, UV — you name it.

To give context for scale — there are 700 unique components on the aircraft designed from scratch by Zipline. We are designing not just the flight computer from scratch, the power distribution board, the motor controllers, the battery, the battery management system, the pod (the smaller robot that we're using to actually make deliveries to people's homes). There's an entire Nvidia GPU-powered flight computer on the pod. We're building the electronics that go into the docking station. Even the electric motor is designed from scratch by Zipline, because we need a thrust-to-weight ratio that is not available in off-the-shelf electric motors.

700 unique components, 43 major subassemblies on the aircraft, all coming together on the manufacturing line and getting assembled into one overall aircraft.

Oftentimes when I talk to people from automotive or aerospace, a common answer to "how do you think about reliability" is: "I ask the supplier what the reliability of the part is." I'm like, "Okay cool. What if we're the supplier?"

So we have component testing on the ground, system testing on the ground where we're taking full aircraft and putting them through vibration tables, wind tunnels, thermal chambers that you can walk into — all of these things in order to understand how this is going to break. More than just "is it good enough" — we want to know how it's going to break, and then we can understand: okay, cool, let's make it better. Let's take this thing to failure. Let's see where the limits are.

从 49°C 到 -25°C：飞在真实世界里

At 49°C, which is very hot, down to -25°C, which is very cold.

We don't fly anywhere at 49 degrees.

We do.

You do? Where?

Phoenix during the summer.

And where's minus 25?

Northern parts of the United States.

Actually, can I ask you guys how you think about that? I could imagine a different version of the world where you guys are like: "Hey look, if it's too hot, we're just not gonna fly. If it's too cold, we're just not going to fly. If it's raining too hard, we're just not going to fly." Obviously your customers would prefer that you fly at all times, but how do you think about those trade-offs?

The easiest way to think about the trade-off was: because of the use cases that Zipline started with, you should — you can count on us with your life and the lives of your loved ones — as long as the sun is shining.

It's just — we developed the capability because we had to for the initial use case.

Zipline would fly. For the first couple years we took a lot of risk. If it's a life-saving delivery happening and there's someone whose life is on the line, we're going to go for it. And we had a civil aviation authority that was generally a great partner with us on that front. We took a lot of risk, we learned a lot, and almost always it worked out. The worst thing that could happen was we had a parachute landing — which is the kind of zipline safety mechanism of last resort — we can pull a parachute on the aircraft and bring it gently to the ground.

安全目标：比 Waymo 安全两倍

To put it into perspective, our original goal was to be 10 times safer than cars. Actually, Alfred was the one pushing in our last board meeting. He's like: "That's a BS goal. We need to be two times safer than Waymo." And so Eric literally went and reset the goal. Now the Zipline target for the end of this year is to be 2x safer than Waymo. He's like, "Cars, that's like archaic technology."

Waymo, I think, is about 10x, right? They're about 10x cars.

Suffice it to say — we now have 140 million commercial autonomous miles and zero safety incidents. Zero. If you were to drive 140 million miles, you would have 600 accidents, 100 injuries, and somewhere between two and six fatalities, depending on what country you're talking about. This is why we really pride ourselves on picking the right use cases — it's life-saving and it really makes a lot of sense to go do it, and also, by God, we're going to be as safe as humanly possible.

One last point — the outcome of all of that testing is we have individual aircraft in the commercial fleet that have flown more than a million commercial autonomous miles. A lot of people look at this and they're like, "Wow, it kind of seems maybe exquisite or fragile — probably very sensitive to extreme conditions or weather." Raise your hand if you have a car that has a million miles on it. These systems are already way more rugged and durable and robust than people necessarily think.

100 米空中的精准送达

Can I ask you about the precision? One of the things that blew my mind when I saw some of the videos — the drone's 100 feet up and it drops the package. It lowers the package to a circle that's got an 18-inch radius or whatever. How do you guys achieve such precision even when it's windy, even when it's raining?

First of all, the aircraft is about 100 meters up. Multiple layers. There's the delivery pod that comes down. The drone is hovering above. It knows where the target is — maybe let's say it's this coffee table. So the aircraft is going to hover above, but it actually needs to consider what the wind conditions are. If the wind's blowing in one direction, then the aircraft is going to be shifted upwind, and it's going to lower that delivery pod down.

We take advantage of GNSS — real-time kinematic GNSS that gives you centimeter-level confidence of where you are. But the thing is: we don't know the GPS coordinates of this table. No one wants to survey the middle of the table and send us the coordinates. So we use that to kind of get close: "Okay, here's the backyard, here's where we roughly know things are." And then the job of this delivery pod is to be lowered down, fight the wind conditions, and be able to use its onboard perception and autonomy systems to identify where's the best place to leave the package. If there's a little table and there's a whole bunch of drinks, probably I shouldn't try and drop down on top of these drinks and make a mess. Maybe I should go to the ground right next to the table.

It has onboard real-time compute to be able to identify what am I looking at, what am I seeing, and how can I find the best place to leave the package — then come down, touch the ground, opens its doors, gets retracted back up, and there you go: the package is left on the ground, and the delivery pod comes back up, stows, and the aircraft flies back home.

A couple big advantages — that pod not only has its own NVIDIA GPU running its own AI autonomy stack so it can survey and know exactly where it's delivering even at night, but it's also controlling its own position in the X and Y axis. So it can not just know but then move.

Advantages of that architecture:

  1. It's quiet. People have this perception — most drones are really freaking annoying. It's basically the most grating annoying sound. Zipline has a big team of aerodynamics, aeroacoustics, and controls experts. Every part of the vehicle is designed with sound in mind. We want it to be no louder than the sound of gentle leaves moving in trees. When the pod is delivering, we're keeping the main aircraft 100 meters in the air — the thing that is creating noise is really far away.

  2. Safety. The only thing that is coming anywhere close to you, your family, your pets, your kids is something that is super cute and safe — a styrofoam kind of anthropomorphic tub.

从卢旺达到达拉斯：进入美国市场的路径

How long was the technology tested outside the United States before you came to the United States?

We spent about eight years — depending on how you measure it, maybe six to eight years. We launched in Rwanda in 2016 our commercial service, and we really launched this next-generation home delivery service — the thing that's now in insane hyperscaling mode — that only launched January of last year. So depending on how you measure it, you could say it was almost nine years.

And when you got to the US, was it smooth sailing? What was the sort of regulatory path?

We really started meaningfully engaging with the US FAA and other regulators around 2020 or so. It was a partnership: our shared goals are safe and efficient airspace integration. We have experience doing that successfully in different countries, we can bring some of that experience in, we have opinions on how this should work — the regulators had opinions on how they thought it should work. It was a partnership over the course of a couple years to identify what those paths looked like and how we could converge and align before we were able to execute.

从"飞行员"到"舰队指挥官"

And you had to show your ability to manage all these aircrafts that are flying.

A huge part of what Zipline's building — the drone is only a part of the overall system. We're really building an infrastructure layer that can enable instant access to products. You don't do that with one aircraft flying from one place to another. You do that with a network of charging locations, hundreds of aircraft spread across an area, with autonomous systems in the cloud that can understand: where am I having demand, where do I have supply, where do I have aircraft, what's coming up (about to be the dinner rush), what's the weather?

There are humans — trained aviation professionals — that are monitoring our network. They're fleet commanders. We used to call them pilots. Because when we originally launched in the US, the first regulatory permission we got was to fly one-to-one — one pilot sitting in an office remote-pilot-in-command basically just observing an aircraft do its thing. Not great for unit economics.

But as Zipline proved out these systems, we went from 1-to-1, to 1-to-3, 1-to-6, 1-to-20, 1-to-40. We're now operating one-to-100 and have plans to go well beyond that.

We technically changed the name because "pilot" is confusing. We're inspired by Ender's Game. We call them fleet commanders. And it actually says that in the FAA documentation — "fleet commanders shall do the following." They are overseeing a group of 100 aircraft.

To me this is the exciting cool thing about technology. People think, "Well, how did humans used to solve this problem?" It's cool how robots enable humans to level up. The human is still strategically managing the system — it's just that the human is now maintaining and commanding robots rather than doing the actual work herself.

每天一百万次配送：新的规模挑战

Now that you guys are kind of in hyperscale mode — what new problems are you running into?

Thematically — getting to two and a half million deliveries — the "oh, it only happens every couple years, it's kind of a one-in-a-million chance." We're on the path towards a million deliveries every day. And if you have a one-in-a-million situation, it's going to happen every single day.

Just to make that clear: it took Zipline from 2014 (when we started building the original version of the technology) to 2024 to do our first million deliveries — maybe the end of 2024, maybe even early 2025. So it was almost a decade to do a million deliveries. Zipline is now, in the very near future, going to be doing a million deliveries a day.

That is definitely humbling. It's like, wow, okay — everything about the way we've been solving the problem is going to break. The bar goes way way up.

Maintenance becomes really hard. The scale of the problems, the number of vehicles that you're managing in the fleet, the cost of a screw-up or if a certain process is operating in very inefficient ways becomes extremely high.

自动化系统的独特优势

There are a lot of ways that these systems operate that I think people don't yet appreciate the advantages of autonomy. One good example: the system wants to operate 24/7 and it does operate 24/7. People are used to logistics generally being "here are the hours when humans are driving trucks." That's not how these systems operate. They can be fully utilized — as happily delivering at 2 a.m. and 3 a.m. delivering something so it's ready for you on your doorstep or in your backyard when you wake up at 6 a.m. before you go to work, as they are delivering at 2 p.m. They can deliver in 5 minutes. They are available 100% of the time.

We are soon going to be flying vehicles straight out of our factory in South San Francisco into commercial operation. If you've seen Tesla Model 3s and Model Ys delivering themselves to customers — Zipline aircraft will fly straight from the factory into operation. Huge advantage from a maintenance perspective: as soon as a vehicle needs to go through proactive maintenance, it will fly itself to the maintenance depot. The human can then do whatever process necessary, and the vehicle flies itself back into operations. We can also dynamically assign capacity in a metro based on what the system is seeing. There's no set home for a vehicle. It can go to wherever it's needed.

To your question about getting to a million a day — the drone is only 15% of the problem. Really, the way that we currently manufacture aircraft, maintain aircraft, support all these things, troubleshoot problems — the way that we do it today isn't going to work when we're at a million deliveries a day. Elon talks about designing the machine that builds the machine. This is really one of the things that I see Zipline tackling over the coming couple years — we're going to be investing much more in the machines that build and run the machines.

一场即将到来的空管革命

From a scale perspective, the largest airline in the US is doing about 5,000 flights a day. Zipline is going to surpass that in the next month. When we get to a million deliveries a day, Zipline will be doing somewhere between 40 and 80 times as many flights in the US in commercial airspace as all other airlines combined.

It's a completely different class of aircraft. It's a totally different kind of problem. But the reality is: when you look at air traffic control, they don't make a distinction. There is a huge transformation that's going to have to happen in air traffic control as we start to realize that people are excited about electrification of vehicles, people are excited about autonomous vehicles — the reality is, as those transformations occur, there are going to be 10 times as many autonomous vehicles in the air as there are using these teeny archaic constrained things that we call roads.

The sky is a big place. It makes sense to utilize it. You can give Earth back to humans. You can make neighborhoods quieter, safer, less pollution, less traffic. This is going to require huge transformation of how we think about air traffic control in the US. We need to design it with AI and autonomy in mind, rather than the way it was designed — which was in 1950 using pencils and paper and NOTAMs and a human looking out trying to watch the airplane.

Are you helping the FAA design that?

What needs to happen is collaborative innovation. One company solving this problem for themselves is not going to solve the problem for the industry. We are heavily involved. A key part of the solution: aircraft should be talking to each other. They should be telling each other where they are, they should be automatically detecting "hey, there's a conflict on the horizon, so you go up, I go down." Our aircraft do that. We're working with other new entrants into the airspace with autonomous aircraft and drones to do the same thing.

We're also working with regulators, working with standards bodies to take some of these best practices and innovations that we and others have developed and try and make them broadly accepted and utilized.

空管系统的隐秘危机

When we were launching in all these other countries, we had to build something from scratch — so we built the thing from scratch. We provided all this software to the civil aviation authorities so that they could use it to monitor this entirely new class of autonomous vehicles in the airspace.

Interestingly, there are multiple public companies in the United States that build air traffic control software that are worth more than $10 billion. There are many companies inside Zipline that are likely — it's like, "oh, that's a public company inside Zipline that's just having to get built from scratch."

Air traffic control is interesting — the more you learn, the more disturbing it is. You read about a plane crashing into a helicopter in DC a few months ago. You read about two planes colliding on a taxiway in an airport. You're like, "Wow, why are all these accidents happening?" Turns out 50% of air traffic controllers are over the age of 45. 20% are about to retire. And nobody is going into air traffic control as a career path right now in the US. There's actually a huge labor crisis around these kinds of jobs. You have pressure coming from different angles — transformation is required. We cannot use a system that was designed for airspace in the 1950s. The labor isn't available to do it even if we wanted to. And also there is this giant influx of new technology — AI and autonomous vehicles — that are going to require us to transform how these systems work.

垂直整合：不是选择，是被迫

So you're a hardware company and a software company. You build, you design your own operations, manufacturing. You design your own parts, you build your own aircraft, you write your own software, you do your own operations. This is a pretty vertically integrated company. Talk about the benefits of complete vertical integration versus buying component parts.

Interestingly, doing it is such an incredible pain in the butt that you would never do it. I have this flag over my desk that says: "We do this not because it is easy, but because we thought that it would be easy." This is definitely the definition of Zipline. It's such a pain in the butt that actually — if you look at the history of all these hardware companies, they all try to not do it first.

Look at the Roadster: "We're going to use a Lotus Elise chassis, we're going to buy the battery pack from a secondary supplier, and we're just going to put the two together." Roadster lost a lot of money and wasn't very reliable — but it was an important part of getting to the Model S.

Zipline when we started — we were buying everything from suppliers. Paying people to design different parts of the system for us, or trying to buy off-the-shelf stuff. And we crashed airplanes. I mean at test sites — we crashed and we crashed. And we realized: wow, this stuff is super expensive and it's also totally unreliable. So part by part you're like: "All right, well, rip that out. We'll design the motor controller from scratch. Rip that out — we're going to have to design the GPS module from scratch, the navigation system."

There's a fundamental realization probably similar to the one that happened that made the Model S possible: if we want to build a really great specific product in this totally new area of technology, we're going to have to design every single one of these components from scratch to meet the specific requirements.

DJI makes plastic quadcopters, and the US buys $20 million Predator aircraft. The reality is both of these systems are very unreliable, and nothing is at a level of reliability and safety at unit economics that would work for this new industry that Zipline was trying to pioneer. We had to build an automotive-grade solution — super reliable and extremely cost-effective, because you're competing against cars and motorcycles which are really cost-effective, and we've had a hundred years to make them reliable and cheap.

You never do it intentionally. Maybe you just slowly freak out and through desperation realize: wow, we got to tear all this out, we got to build it all from scratch.

沙丁鱼办公室：整合团队的力量

The advantage of doing it from scratch is speed and integration. When you visit Zipline's offices, we are all absolutely packed like sardines into this small building. You have firmware engineers sitting next to mechanical engineers sitting next to autonomy engineers sitting next to cloud infra sitting next to aeroacoustics, guidance, navigation, controls, systems engineering, manufacturing — everything, everywhere, in one place. Our factory is a three-minute drive away. Our team is on the factory floor working, seeing parts get integrated into the overall system. Our test sites are just a short drive away.

Combining all these things together means stuff is always breaking, stuff's always going wrong. The advantage is: when the thing goes wrong, we can basically go straight to the person's desk and be like, "You and I are pulling an all-nighter tonight." Whereas if you're Boeing and something's going wrong with the battery on the 787, you're suing a supplier and taking two years to try to figure out whose fault it is. It's three layers deep in the rat's nest of how these procurement deals and supply chains work.

Getting these different-discipline folks to work together — honestly it's quite easy when you have that setup. Everyone's rowing in the same direction, we all have the same goals, and when you can ground it in reality and it's tangible, then we're all just here to solve the same problems. With vertical integration and a diverse team, we cut through a lot of the stuff. You don't have "oh, that engineer won't tell me what the source code does because they said it's IP, and so we don't actually know what the fault detection looks like." You just literally go walk over, sit next to the person's desk: "Hey, we failed that test. Tell me about how this part of the system works." "Oh cool, pull up the code. Great, let's look through it. Oh interesting, you're making that assumption. That's not how I designed it. Cool, let's get to the bottom of it."

质疑每一个需求，删除每一个零件

It really makes you realize when you build complex AI and robotic systems that combine hardware and software — you really appreciate the deep religious truth of how dumb requirements usually are.

Question every requirement — which is the number one part of Elon's algorithm they talk about at SpaceX. This is so profoundly and deeply true. You must have every team question every requirement. The requirement is always stupid when — you go to this team and that team, you often have to dig two levels deep to realize this is —

The other thing is delete the part. The most reliable part on an aircraft is the part that is not on the aircraft at all, because you deleted it in the last design. That part will never fail. You take a lot of inspiration from looking at the Raptor 1, Raptor 2, Raptor 3 engines next to each other. A lot of people who come to the factory now get to see the EV3 aircraft, the EV2 aircraft, the EV1 aircraft, plus the 10 different hardware versions we built on the first version of Zipline's technology. You just delete, delete, delete.

It's really hard to delete things. It's an act of courage. No one wants to delete the thing. You look like an idiot if you delete the thing and then the system can't perform or doesn't work because you deleted the thing. But true confidence in the physics and the performance of the system enables you to start deleting things. It's a big advantage of having full integrated control of all of these systems — it makes it possible to question every requirement, it makes it possible to delete parts.

一个"删除尾钩"的故事

First principles thinking is a huge part of that. I remember the Platform One aircraft early days — it had a deployable tail hook. It had this big hook, like a meter long, that would come down from the aircraft, and we had a line that would catch that and slow the airplane down as this kind of complicated contraption.

We had this idea that we should be able to move that complexity to the ground systems, and have the recovery system, the landing system, more like an aircraft carrier — grab the airplane. We can put the actuation on. Basically a robot that goes up and grabs the airplane. And we're like, man, that's going to make the aircraft so much simpler, so much lighter, so much more reliable. We didn't have it working yet, and it was time to build that next generation of the aircraft. And we're like: so, do we build them with the meter-long tail hook, or do we delete the tail hook and put the two-centimeter-long tail hook on the back and bet that we can get this thing working?

We got in a room, we're like: delete it. Let's do this thing. From first principles it should work. We can make it work. We haven't done it yet, but we can do it. The next couple weeks looked like — myself included — a lot of people pulling a lot of late nights getting that thing working. And sure enough, those first aircraft came and we caught them and landed them. It's a lot of courage, but really being grounded in first principles thinking with a tight integrated team is how you do that.

送人？暂时不要问

Is there a version of the future in which, instead of delivering life-saving medicine and cheeseburgers, you're delivering human beings?

Uh oh.

Hand the board member that has to control their costs.

I mean, safe, reliable, battle-tested. If we're going to liberate ourselves from the tyranny of streets, it's a pretty decent solution.

Gosh, I think I agree with you. I'm measured in the way I answer that question, because to be clear: building a new infrastructure layer for the planet that can deliver packages as efficiently as the internet moves information is going to be one of the biggest companies on Earth. It's a huge opportunity, and we definitely want to stay humble and paranoid about how super hard that's going to be.

市场比你想象的大得多

We now have many partners who are each asking to buy a million deliveries a day of capacity from Zipline in the last few months. Our operating plan has become our unit of sale. We had originally sized the entire factory to build 20,000 aircraft a year — that was about what was required for a million a day. All of this is being thrown — we're realizing the market is way bigger.

There are 5.5 billion instant deliveries being done by humans in the United States every year — and we're using a 4,000-pound gas combustion vehicle to do it.

Instant — it's like half an hour to an hour.

Exactly. It's good marketing that it's called "instant" — but 30 minutes, 45 minutes, an hour. A significant percentage of the drivers report eating some of the food that they've delivered in the last month — more than 50%. There are significant safety concerns.

5.5 billion instant deliveries. What we're realizing when you look at Zipline now at massive scale in Dallas — we're now launching four more metros in the next four months — if you were to extend the buying behavior that we're observing from Zipline customers in Dallas to the rest of the United States, there would be 55 billion instant deliveries happening, not 5.

It's similar to how people looked at Uber when they were launching in San Francisco: "Oh, even if Uber gets to be 33% of the taxi market in San Francisco, it's only going to be a $15 billion company." Obviously what they missed is: Uber is now 10 times the size of the taxi market. If you make something more convenient and less expensive and a better product experience, people are going to consume a lot more of it.

We are clearly seeing customer behavior where customers order every day rather than a couple times a month. I met a grandma the other day who's ordered 350 times from Zipline in the last year. She's 80 years old.

Amazing.

Nursing homes are big Zipline demand centers. People perceive old people as maybe not capable of using technology — they're all living on their iPhones. They're probably doom scrolling actually, which is maybe not a good thing — but they are very comfortable using Apple ID or Apple Pay, Face ID, and just ordering and having it delivered directly to them.

There are definitely not enough humans in the United States to do 55 billion deliveries. The only way we're going to be able to serve this kind of demand is with automated systems. And there's definitely not enough roads. When you look at traffic in most of our major cities — "Oh, can we just double the number of cars on the roads so we can do way more deliveries?" — it obviously doesn't work. We actually need to be taking cars off the roads if we want to enable human growth and flourishing. This change is inevitable.

数字与单位经济学

How many flights are you doing a day now, and how many will you do in a month?

Zipline is now doing almost 5,000 flights a day. We're anticipating exiting this year at above 30,000 flights a day, and our goal is to get to a million flights a day as fast as humanly possible, which we expect to achieve in the very near future.

The things that are interesting to think about on the unit economics front: whenever we meet hardware companies, they always talk about how much do you think the system is going to cost, and they're always like, "It's going to cost X." And you're like, "Cool. It's going to cost 10X — just so you know — when you build it, it's going to cost 10X."

That's your advice to founders?

That's my advice to founders. For hardware companies: assume it's going to cost 10 times that. Does it work? What would you do if it cost 10 times that?

We're speaking from experience. When we launched our system in 2016, we were charging $30 a delivery to deliver a blood transfusion over 80 to 100 miles. That was cost-comparable, so we signed the contract for it. We thought we were going to launch a system that cost about $30 a delivery. How much do you think it cost when we launched?

$300.

Yeah, it cost $300 a delivery. Alfred was surprisingly chill about it. We were like, "All right, we got work to do." The next year we got it to 120. Then 75. Then 40. Then 28. Then 18. It's now 12 for the kind of long-range technology that we operate outside the US.

一个安静的、正在到来的临界点

What's happening this summer is: the fully burdened unit economics of these systems is just now in the process of falling below the cost of using cars to deliver things. It's a cool moment that most people don't really realize. It's happening quietly. You're not reading about this in the New York Times. But this thing is happening in the next month or two that is going to have a big impact on the world.

Because it is now more cost-effective to use a robot in logistics than it is to use a human. And that's really good news for the environment. It's really good news for neighborhoods that are going to get quieter and safer and less traffic, less pollution. And it's really good news for customers because you can get things way faster and more reliably and for less expensive.

Our customers love — obviously so many cool things about the system — but "no tip!!!" is like the number one comment. Customers love not having to feel guilty and being able to have a system that they know how much it's going to cost.

尾声：拥抱黑暗，重返基础设施建设

Well, thank you, Keller and Eric, for being here with us. I thought you were going to say it takes longer than you thought, not 10x more than it costs.

It does also take a lot longer. The memo that Sean wrote here at Sequoia a few years ago I think is deeply true. I don't know if he'll ever publish that publicly — but there is an internal Sequoia memo that has had a big impact on me, talking about (a) why hardware companies are going to be some of the most impactful companies for humanity's progress over the coming decades; (b) why it's super hard to get those companies off the ground and fundraise for them; and (c) how investors should think about funding those kinds of companies.

When you look at the world today — wow, how fast the world changes. We spent 10 years being the freaking black sheep — "hardware company, no thank you, let's invest in SaaS, let's invest in margins, this is where the whole future was, iPhone apps." I guess I feel like — you know Bane in Batman? What does he say? "You adopted the darkness — I was born in it." We built a robotics company for 10 years before building a robotics company was a cool thing to do.

Especially important for US competitiveness — and just for our ability to build the future that we'd be really proud to hand to our kids and grandkids, to build the sci-fi version of the future that we were all promised — we've got to get good at building stuff again. Not just vehicles and hardware. We got to get good at building infrastructure. We're depending on the crumbling infrastructure that our grandparents built for us.

I read the other day: we just installed these anti-suicide nets on the Golden Gate Bridge. Have you guys read about that project? It cost more to install those nets than it cost our grandparents to build that bridge.

I believe it.

We think the promising future is: we should be able to build infrastructure. We have to be interested in it. People have to have the stomach for it. We have to learn how to manufacture and run complex supply chains again. And we have to be bold and believe in sci-fi versions of the future if we're going to build them.

Awesome. Let's end it at that. Believe in the sci-fi future.

Yeah.

Thank you guys for being with us.

Thank you. Thanks for inviting us.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"15%悖论"：被低估的隐形基础设施
Keller反复强调：**飞机只占整个解决方案复杂度的15%**。这是本次对话中最反直觉的商业洞察——它颠覆了几乎所有硬件叙事。
真正震撼的是：Zipline在早期把飞机当作99%的答案，结果签了21家医院却只服务了1家，工程师在Rwanda凌晨集体崩溃。这个"15%教训"其实是**所有硬件公司都要付的学费**——从Tesla Roadster用Lotus底盘失败，到SpaceX不得不自建每一颗螺丝。硬件产品的可见部分永远只是冰山，真正决定成败的是**看不见的85%**：库存管理、民航整合、需求预测、维修网络……
这里有一个隐藏的、几乎没人指出的连接：**这15%/85%的比例，与LLM时代惊人一致**。今天所有做AI应用的公司都在重复Zipline的错误——以为模型就是产品。GPT-4只是那15%，剩下85%是评估管道、上下文工程、身份认证、失败恢复、监管应对。**Zipline在没有大模型的十年里，替整个AI应用行业提前跑通了这道题。**
## 二、"病人不分昼夜"：产品市场契合度的黑暗版本
Eric讲的Rwanda故事表面

item.title: [Lex Clips] Lessons from the Roman Empire for modern-day - historian explains | Anthony Kaldellis
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：罗马军队之所以"言行一致"，恰恰因为它没有现代金融

Kaldellis 说了一句可能被读者滑过去的话——"in pre-modern context, it's very difficult to operate shadow foreign policies"。这不是道德问题，而是技术—金融基础设施问题。

罗马皇帝之所以能让"军队保护子民"这一叙事高度对应现实，不是因为古人更诚实，而是因为： - 没有远程转账、没有影子公司、没有承包商链条 - 军费开支的"物质流"和"信息流"几乎重叠——粮食走哪、军团去哪、皇帝说什么，三者物理上耦合 - 想撒谎，成本极高；想执行，路径极短

而现代美国"言行差距"的根源不是政客变坏了，而是金融—信息基础设施让"说"和"做"第一次可以完全解耦。军事承包商、SPV、离岸账户、媒体叙事——这些让"宣称目的"与"实际资金流向"变成了两条独立轨道。

换句话说：Machiavelli 想让言行一致的建议，在拜占庭是常识，在华盛顿在物理上就困难。

二、跨领域连接：Kaldellis 的"文化调旋钮 1-10"模型 = 进化心理学 + 产品 A/B 测试

他反驳后现代主义时提出了一个极其漂亮的模型：人性参数是固定区间（1 到 10），文化只是调节旋钮的位置。

这个模型出奇地同构于三个完全不同的领域： - 进化心理学的"人性普适性"论（Tooby & Cosmides） - 产品设计中的可配置参数（feature flags）——底层数据模型不变，UI 让不同用户看到不同权重 - 大型语言模型的 temperature/top-p——同一个模型，不同采样参数，产生截然不同的输出分布

惊喜之处：这意味着"罗马人"和"美国人"不是不同物种，而是同一模型在不同 temperature 下的采样结果。历史学家研究的其实是人性的超参数搜索空间。

而 Steve Jobs、Musk、"戴滑稽帽子的家伙"（他显然暗指希特勒）——这些人不是"超出人性"，而是在旋钮通常锁死的位置上，成功地拧动了它。他们是历史上的 outlier prompt engineers。

三、被访谈双方都没说破的深层结构："制度寿命"与"领导人任期"的失配是所有民主帝国的根本病灶

Lex 提到"民主的短视是问题"，Kaldellis 说"我也不想被皇帝统治"。两人礼貌地绕开了真正的矛盾：

罗马能建造"跨千年的道路和教会"，是因为它的决策者的时间视野大于人类寿命。皇帝把王朝、家族、身后名当作决策变量。

美国的四年任期让每个决策者的"折现率"极高——任何超过 4 年才见效的投资，在博弈论上都是负收益。这不是政客的道德问题，是激励结构的时间常数错配。

最反直觉的推论：越南战争之所以能"惩罚"军事领导层几十年，是因为伤害是代际记忆；而 Constantinople 不需要这种惩罚机制，因为它的制度记忆本身就是跨代的。民主必须靠"创伤"来实现罗马靠"延续"就能实现的东西。

四、一个隐藏的系统学真相：帝国的稳定性 ≈ 叙事—物质耦合度

如果把上面三点合成一个公式：

帝国韧性 = (制度时间常数 × 言行耦合度) / 领导人折现率

罗马：分子巨大（千年制度 × 高言行耦合），分母小（皇帝寿命=时间视野）→ 极稳。 美国：分子被军工复合体和 4 年任期同时侵蚀，分母被选举周期放大 → 需要不断"觉醒—纠偏"循环来维持。

Kaldellis 没说的是：这个公式对公司、对开源社区、对宗教组织同样成立。这就是为什么苹果在 Jobs 死后能延续、维基百科能自我修正、而大多数创业公司在创始人退出 4 年内消散——本质上是同一个方程。

📝 转录文本

America as Empire — a once-taboo framing

Lex: So what from all of this, from this 2200 year history of the Roman Empire, what lessons can we draw for modern times? Now we're sitting in America, let's see, young empire. [snorts] I know we're not calling it an empire, but you know—

Kaldellis: Lots of people are calling it an empire.

Lex: I thought it was sort of taboo. Yeah, in the 20th century it was like, is America an empire or not? But like I think since the Iraq war, like since 2003, I I I've seen at least very casual uses of America and empire in the same, you know, sentence without any kind of hand-wringing.

Lex: Is it ever called that in academic setting?

Kaldellis: Yes.

Lex: I mean it's technically I guess you could say given the expanse of the military bases and the military might and the way that represents itself in the geopolitical context.

Kaldellis: There are books published all the time that unironically discuss America as an empire in various ways.

Lex: By the way, both positive and negative. So when you're saying a positive thing about America calling it an empire and when you're saying it to criticize America you call it an empire. Yeah, you're right.

Kaldellis: Yeah, I mean most academic books aren't trying to do like either, they're just trying to analyze the situation.

Lesson 1 — Institutions built to last

Lex: What lessons about representing the people, about flourishing, about stability can we draw?

Kaldellis: I will say that, just drawing on what we were talking about a moment ago, that investing in institutions that work for the majority of your subjects — even if indirectly, like they have to pay a cost for them, but you have to explain to them why they're paying that cost — is to the benefit of the ruling classes as well. Because ideally you're thinking about long-term success and not just your own short-term gain.

And I think that's what this society tends to do well. You know, the Romans generally, how they built roads for the centuries and the millennia, they built institutions too. One of the institutions they built was like the Christian church. They build things to last. And I think that's a good lesson beyond even the roads. I think institutions that explain to people why they exist — yeah, I think we could use some of that.

Lesson 2 — Closing the gap between rhetoric and action

Lex: And also the first point you made about the East Roman Empire, I think you've said that US foreign policy sometimes has a quite a large gap between the rhetoric and the action in modern times.

Kaldellis: Yeah.

Lex: And so this to Machiavelli's point that we want to maybe narrow that gap for a successful empire, for a successful nation, the rhetoric has to match the action.

Kaldellis: Yes.

Lex: And it doesn't quite for some of the foreign policy engagements in the world.

Kaldellis: So, in the context of the society I study, let's say the army is the single largest expense — is doing pretty much what the emperor says that it's doing, which is protecting its subjects. That's actually what it's doing most of the time. Not all the time. So, for example, emperors will sometimes take the armies out on a raid or an expedition, you know, crack some skulls, gather some plunder, come back, and this is done to glorify the emperor. Yeah, yeah, this does happen. But for the most part, they're doing that, not much else.

And I've actually — in a pre-modern context, it's very difficult to operate shadow foreign policies and things like this. It's just not part of the — it's not in the cards. But America has such a history of proclaiming the loftiest goals. It just sometimes just, you know, breathtakingly implausible.

Lex: Plus the incredible engineering behind the military-industrial complex. So America's very good at creating —

Kaldellis: Yeah.

Lex: — the flamethrowers.

Kaldellis: Yes.

Lex: The technologies of war. And so the lofty goals combined with just a lot of profit that can be made on the technologies of war creates a very difficult situation to navigate where the gap naturally widens.

Kaldellis: I don't think most of the world even believes what American leaders say their goals are, especially when it comes to like war. Like spreading democracy. Really? I remember when they were trying to convince us that the invasion of Afghanistan was a feminist war.

Lex: Oh, that was a thing, huh?

Kaldellis: Oh, yes. They were to liberate the women from the burkas or whatever. And the news was just flooded with like how terrible women have it in Afghanistan. It's like, you really trying to persuade me that that's why you're sending armed for like — [sighs] Anyway.

Lex: I think there was a lot a lot of wake-up calls about the war in Afghanistan, Iraq where people realized that there is that gap between the rhetoric and the action. And I mean, that's how — because of a lot of people waking up to that they create pressure on the government to close the gap. And that's how we kind of navigate this complicated world, but it's a costly one. I mean, these were extremely costly wars on every front, on every measure.

Kaldellis: Yep, but the same lesson was learned in like the Vietnam War. And that was such a disaster that essentially they had to switch to — the conscript army, switch to a professional army. And it took them so long to be able to wage these empire wars again because of the sheer damage that the Vietnam war did to the credibility of the military leadership in the country, right? This never happens in Constantinople. You can be criticized for losing a war. That's one thing, right? But that you're using the army to do some like, you know, no.

Lesson 3 — Democracy's short-termism vs. imperial time horizons

Lex: Some of this does have to do with the functioning of how democracy works, the temporary nature of the leaderships, and that they're very short-sided because of the nature of the elected position of the executive. I mean, that's what we're trying to figure out as a human civilization, how to do this — in this particular case, the democracy thing.

Kaldellis: There's no right answer. And by the way, I don't want to be ruled by kings or monarchs or emperors, either. So it's like I wouldn't necessarily support that. I'm not advocating here for anything. I'm just trying to explain. But you're exactly right. We want democracy, we want leaders whom we can eject every four or five years, which includes sometimes they're teams of experts and policy designers and whatever. And that comes at a cost, too.

Lesson 4 — Is there such a thing as "human nature"?

Lex: So, you said that, you know, the Roman Empire, maybe ancient Greece, maybe the societies before that and now societies after that, the society we live in are very different. And so applying the lessons of history, you have to be careful doing so. But are there things that are the same with us humans? So, of everything you've studied across hundreds of years and centuries, are there aspects of human nature that kind of persist?

Kaldellis: So, look, intellectually, I have gone through periods when — like when I was a grad student, it was, you know, this was kind of when post-modernism, what we might call loosely, was kind of alive and well and had not yet been kicked in the pants. It was very common to find colleagues who did not believe that there was such a thing as, let's say, human nature. In other words, that the parameters of human psychology are — yes, there's considerable variation, but they're within a certain set of norms. Like we're not going to behave like different species. And that they did not believe that there was such a thing. In other words, that all of human history is essentially a series of incommensurate slices of difference, right? That you just couldn't go from one to the next.

And I think it's a very problematic view for all kinds of reasons. Like how small do you slice these slices? I mean it — and no, I'm with Thucydides on this one, who says that, you know, more or less human beings are going to more or less — they're going to be after, right? They love and they hate and they have ambitions and they're incompetent and, you know, you find the whole range of, you know, the whole gamut of diversity pretty much in every — any group of about 20 or 30 people, you're going to find the same kinds of types.

Now, culture then comes in and, you know, tweaks things in all kinds of weird ways. And that's, as a historian, what I like to study. I like just how the culture will modify, will set the dials, right? But that the dials go from one to 10.

Lesson 5 — The exceptional individuals who turn the tide

Lex: Most people is like, yeah, yeah, yeah, I think so. But every once in a while there'll be a few folks who come along, whether it's Steve Jobs or Elon Musk or some guy with a weird mustache or with a funny hat — and those can turn the tides of history.

Kaldellis: I think so. Occasionally, yes.

Lex: But they're just ultra skilled at the right time at the right moment.

Kaldellis: Oh, I believe that, too. Oh, yeah. In other words, I don't think that cultural systems or ideologies are so effectively totalitarian that they sort of colonize the mind of every person. And so everyone in a culture is just like a clone. No. You encounter this sometimes in historical scholarship.

Lex: Mhm.

Kaldellis: And no, I think people can sometimes just think for themselves or they can think outside the box and they can do all kinds of things. And I always want to keep my mind open to that possibility when I'm encountering historical agents.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：罗马军队之所以"言行一致"，恰恰因为它没有现代金融
Kaldellis 说了一句可能被读者滑过去的话——**"in pre-modern context, it's very difficult to operate shadow foreign policies"**。这不是道德问题，而是**技术—金融基础设施**问题。
罗马皇帝之所以能让"军队保护子民"这一叙事高度对应现实，不是因为古人更诚实，而是因为： - 没有远程转账、没有影子公司、没有承包商链条 - 军费开支的"物质流"和"信息流"几乎重叠——粮食走哪、军团去哪、皇帝说什么，三者物理上耦合 - 想撒谎，成本极高；想执行，路径极短
而现代美国"言行差距"的根源不是政客变坏了，而是**金融—信息基础设施让"说"和"做"第一次可以完全解耦**。军事承包商、SPV、离岸账户、媒体叙事——这些让"宣称目的"与"实际资金流向"变成了两条独立轨道。
换句话说：**Machiavelli 想让言行一致的建议，在拜占庭是常识，在华盛顿在物理上就困难**。
## 二、跨领域连接：Kaldellis 的"文化调

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Math Problem That Fooled Terry Tao – Grant Sanderson (@3blue1brown )
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被"上下文"困住的不只是学生，也是所有专家

这段对话最锋利的一刀，其实不是关于 IMO，而是关于上下文本身就是一种智力枷锁。

学生在 IMO 现场之所以被"钓鱼题"戏弄，不是因为智商不够，而是因为他们过度拟合了"IMO 应有的解法气质"——优雅、精巧、层层递进。可若把同一道题作为脑筋急转弯抛给街上的陌生人，反而更可能秒解。这里藏着一个反直觉的真相：训练得越好的头脑，越容易在特定语境中被自己的先验困死。Terry Tao 之所以栽跟头，恰恰是因为他"太像一个 IMO 选手"了。

这让人联想到医学史上一个有趣的现象——资深放射科医生有时会漏诊那些"太明显"的病灶，因为他们的注意力被训练去寻找微妙线索。专业化的代价，是对"愚蠢答案"的失明。

二、AI 的救赎可能不在"更聪明"，而在"可编程的偏见"

对话中真正让我兴奋的转折是：他们把"AI 都长一个样"这个常被诟病的缺陷，翻转成了一个可以被工程化利用的优势。

人类科学史上，Einstein 靠"参照系不变性"的偏见推出相对论，也靠"上帝不掷骰子"的偏见拒绝了量子力学。偏见既是发动机，也是刹车片。而人类无法选择自己的偏见——它由童年、文化、导师塑造，几乎不可拆卸。

但 AI 可以。你可以同时开 100 个 agent，每个装载不同的先验：一个坚信猜想为真、一个坚信为假；一个像 Einstein、一个像 Bohr；一个用组合方法、一个用代数几何。这不是"更聪明"，而是把认识论的多样性从"社会学问题"降维成了"prompt engineering 问题"。

跨领域连接：这与进化生物学中的"有性生殖为何胜出无性生殖"高度同构——不是因为有性生殖的个体更强，而是因为基因组合的熵更高，抗打击面更广。AI 的未来可能不是训练一个超级模型，而是构建一个认识论意义上的有性生殖系统。

三、真正被暴露的：科学没有"正确的启发式"

最出人意料的观察藏在对话的最后一句，说出来时甚至有点漫不经心："科学没有正确的启发式，你需要多个独立的研究纲领各自带着自己的偏见并行推进。"

这句话把整个 AI-for-science 的叙事悄悄拆解了。主流叙事是"AI 会加速科学，因为它更快更准"。但这段对话暗示的是一个更颠覆的图景：AI 加速科学的机制不是"算得更快"，而是"能同时活成不同的哲学立场"。

这里有一个值得警惕的推论——如果一家实验室（OpenAI、Anthropic、DeepMind）的所有模型都用相似的语料、相似的 RLHF、相似的价值观训练出来，那么它们在集体意义上，就是一个 Einstein。它们会在同一个地方一起卡住，一起对量子力学摇头。

真正的 AI 科研突破，可能不来自最强的那个模型，而来自最不像其他模型的那个模型。这意味着未来 AI 竞争的关键指标，也许不是 benchmark 分数，而是"模型间的认知距离"——一个几乎还没有人在测量的维度。

📝 转录文本

引子：一道让 Terry Tao 也栽了的 IMO 题

In this uh like AI and math series, the first episode will do a will be about like when they solve the IMO. And I want to focus on one specific IMO problem that they failed on, which is one that a lot of very smart students failed on. Terry Tao also failed on it.

Um and the nature of it is basically that it — people were very mad at the problem because they called it a troll problem. I almost don't want to spoil it because I I want to construct the episode around like leading someone in with um without knowing that it turns out to have a simple solution, because you like can really empathize with what it's like to be like a student solving this.

"钓鱼题"的结构：优雅的死胡同

Basically, there's a really elegant way of going down what you really feel like is going to be the solution based on the context of being the international math olympiad problem positioned as it is. The like character of the solution is like really enticing, but it's kind of hard to prove that it's the best.

The reason is that it's not. There's like this almost brain-dead solution that is the best.

逃离上下文：这道题真正考的是什么

And so, the like relevance of that to the whole AI story is like — for a human, what's required to answer that question is to like escape your context. Escape the context that you're in the IMO. Escape the context of the way you've been trained to solve these like contest math problems.

Um and if you just approached it like a like a brain teaser that I throw someone off the street, like they'd probably answer it well. And you sort of want the same sometimes for like uh uh like human research in in other contexts, where like sometimes just being able to say: refresh your thinking, come at it completely differently.

数字心智的可能优势：系统化地"刷新思维"

So, of all the advantages that digital minds have, that might actually be one of them — like a little bit more of a systematic what does it look like to like refresh your thinking, try to answering two separate questions, like spin off two agents, one who's trying to prove it, one who's trying to disprove it. One who tries it like this way, one who tries it — and they like deliberately have different contexts.

I I would be curious to see if we're having this conversation 3 years from now, how many of the like significant results that make headlines have that character of basically like erasing the context previously, like trying a bunch of different things as opposed to merging the results of like a bunch of different —

熵坍缩 vs. 系统化的多样性

Anything incredibly interesting, because a common concern people have about AIs is this entropy collapse where they all think the same way because they're trained in similar ways. This is why they're bad at writing. They kind of just like go down the same path and have similar patterns of speaking and so forth.

But maybe actually the key advantage AIs have is that you can systematically — it sounded like one of the reasons the unit distance problem conjecture took so long to be disproven — which because people assumed the conjecture was actually true. So they were mostly they were trying to figure out ways in which to prove it.

And so maybe one of the key advantages AIs will have is actually to increase the entropy by systematically trying out both the negation and trying to prove the positive of any given statement, of like — or being able to like systematically give different agents different biases.

That's a good point.

Einstein 的启示：偏见既是燃料也是陷阱

Like it seems like an important thing in the history of human science is that like Einstein is just really motivated by this bias — that like things should look the same in different reference frames. And then he had multiple other biases like these. But like that is just a very formative in his thinking, and you can just like systematically survey a bunch of heuristics and see which ones are being productive at a given problem.

Yeah. And so you would suggest basically like systematically increasing entropy at the prompt level, even though you have this like inevitable collapse at the like auto regression level.

Yeah. That mean — And and I mean Einstein would be an interesting example, because it's like he's got this bias towards things should be — He also has a bias towards like God should not play dice. Right? And it's almost like you — you want to make sure that you don't accidentally have all of your LLMs are Einstein, because you might halt on quantum mechanics progress. Right?

结论：科学没有"正确的启发式"

Actually goes to show you that you — there's not a correct heuristic for science. You actually just need multiple independent research programs with their own heuristics.

Yeah. Yeah.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
