item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Brent Johnson: Strong DOLLAR is Bullish For GOLD
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察：黄金下跌恰恰证明了它作为"储备资产"的成功

多数分析者把最近几个月黄金回调解读为"debasement trade 失败"，Brent 却揭示了一个被完全忽视的镜像逻辑：黄金正因为"太成功"而下跌。当俄罗斯、土耳其、海湾国家被迫抛售黄金去换美元，这不是黄金失宠的证据，而是黄金已经替代了美国国债的储备地位的证据——只有真正被当作储备资产的东西，才会在危机中被首先动用。

换句话说：黄金的下跌，是它取代美债过程中的"服役伤"，不是"退役信号"。 这与保险公司的资产负债表逻辑完全同构——保险公司越是被大量理赔，越说明它的产品被信任、被广泛持有。危机中被抛售的资产不是失败者，而是流动性最好的赢家。

二、跨领域连接：石油"价格带"与货币"价格带"的同构——一个热力学隐喻

Brent 借用《Land Man》里油价"带状区间"的比喻是精彩的，但更深的洞察在于：美元指数的"band"其实是一个耗散结构，就像生物体的体温调节——37°C 上下 2°C 是舒适区，超过则触发系统性反馈。

这里的意外连接是：美元的机制类似于抗生素的耐药性演化。每一轮 QE（"抗生素"）都会短期压低美元、注入流动性，但同时创造出更大规模的美元债务（"耐药菌"），等到药效退去，反噬更猛烈。所以真正的问题不是"美元会不会崩"，而是每次救市都在把下一次危机的量级放大一个数量级。这也解释了为什么去美元化"意愿极高、能力极低"——就像抗药性问题人人皆知，却没人能真正停用抗生素。

三、被忽视的权力转移：稳定币是财政部对美联储的"静默政变"

这是全场最容易被读者错过的一句话——Brent 说"stable coins give the Treasury a tool they never had before"。表面上这是技术升级，实质上是美国货币权力结构的重心迁移。

传统架构下：财政部 → 美联储 → 商业银行 → 公众。稳定币架构下：财政部 → 稳定币发行商 → 智能手机 → 全球任何人。银行系统作为分发层被绕过了。

这里有一个惊人的跨领域类比：这与 Netflix 绕过有线电视网络、Uber 绕过出租车调度系统、Substack 绕过传统出版社的逻辑完全一致——都是"分发中介的去中介化"。而当分发中介被削弱，原来依附于该中介的监管权、议价权、政治权也随之瓦解。这也解释了为什么小型社区银行会成为最先倒下的一批——它们相当于货币界的"独立书店"。

更深一层：当财政部可以绕过美联储直达全球用户时，"央行独立性"这个战后神话就走到尽头了。而这个终结不是通过政治斗争完成的，而是通过一个技术协议悄悄完成的——权力的最大转移，往往发生在没人意识到那是权力转移的时候。

四、彩蛋：欧洲美元是全世界自己造的监狱

Brent 那句"the Eurodollar market is a prison the rest of the world built for themselves over the last 50 years"值得反复咀嚼。这与心理学上的"习得性无助"、组织学上的"路径依赖锁定"、甚至热力学上的"熵增不可逆"共享同一种结构——最坚固的束缚从来不是外部强加的，而是使用者自己一砖一瓦砌起来的。这也是为什么金砖国家 20 年反美元收效甚微：他们同时在拆墙和砌墙。

📝 转录文本

Brent Johnson：强势美元长期利多黄金

开场：核心论点

The dollar getting stronger is probably the most bullish thing long-term for gold that there is, because a strong dollar will destroy the global economy. The global system is not designed for a strong dollar.

The desire to de-dollarize I think is extremely high. The ability to de-dollarize I think is extremely low.

We are moving towards a time where the Fed and the Treasury are going to come into conflict. And if it comes to a big fight, the Treasury will win — because stablecoins to a certain extent give the Treasury a tool that they never had before.

There's stablecoin wars going on between countries, and there's between the banking system and the stablecoin issuers. This is a huge deal, and I think this is a way they can potentially in the future get more control over the Eurodollar market.

主持人开场

Welcome to the Microscopic Podcast. My name is Alex Adronov and I'm your host. Today is the 1st of July 2026 and I have the honor of welcoming back Brent Johnson. Welcome back, Brent.

Brent: Thanks for having me. It's good to be here.

Alex: Last time we saw each other was in Cape Town about three, four months ago already. Time flies, huh?

Brent: That was such a great trip and it was so much fun to see your organization all hanging out together. It was a great time. So thanks again for having me. I appreciate it.

Alex: Was our pleasure. It was a great time. I agree. Unfortunately, we cannot reproduce this now. This is the background — that's all the background we're going to give the viewers today.

一、六个月回顾：Debasement Trade 的反转

Alex: The point of this conversation is a bit to follow up on your thesis — the milkshake, the stable milkshake. I know it's evolving so much. I was making a joke — the golden stable milkshake at this point would be the way I would maybe resume it.

In your latest newsletter, you described the update six months later of the debasement trade: the consensus that six months ago gold and silver would go to the moon, the dollar would collapse — and that it actually completely unwound, with the dollar breaking a 13-month high while gold and other hard assets sold off quite sharply recently. You also noted that in the long-term thesis this may still hold, but the path is actually a painful path of corrections.

So what do you see as the key mechanical driver behind this recent shift, and where do you see also stablecoins fit into the dollar's ongoing strength?

Brent 的回应：长期看多，但路径痛苦

Brent: This is really important to understand. I want to be clear as I lay this out: I still believe hard assets, commodities, gold, stores of value should be owned in a portfolio, and I think they're going to go much higher in the years ahead because I think fiat currency does get debased. But the rate at which the different currencies get debased and the relative level of fiat currencies is extremely important.

Many people have said you can have a decade-long bull market but experience a 50% drawdown at some point along the way. A lot of my work explaining the dollar mechanics is largely to explain why those drawdowns happen and some of the things you can do to understand why they happen and perhaps even protect against them.

If you don't have the time frame of 10 years to wait for this debasement trade to play off, or you don't want to go through a 50% drawdown along the way, or if you're interested in something other than just gold — if you're interested in geopolitics, capital markets, asset prices — then understanding the dollar mechanics are extremely important.

"The Band" 报告

I know this is a long answer, but I actually just wrote a paper on this as well. We've written a paper that kind of explains the mechanics of this about as well as I think I can. I've talked a lot about the dollar over the years — I haven't written a lot about the dollar. But we wrote a paper recently called The Band. If anybody's interested, they can go to research.santiagocap.com.

二、"The Band" 理论：美元的价格带

灵感来源：《Land Man》

The reason I call it The Band — I don't know if you've ever heard of the TV show Land Man, but there's a scene where Billy Bob Thornton explains why the price of oil has to stay within this band. If the price of oil gets too high, it causes a lot of problems in the world. If it gets too weak, it causes a lot of problems in the world.

I used that as an analogy because I think the exact same thing happens with the dollar. The whole world uses the dollar on the global stage. The dollar is the global reserve currency. The Eurodollar market is the market through which the world trades with each other. The whole world has to use dollars.

美元创造的机制：贷款而非印钞

The issue is that the way you get dollars into the system is they are loaned into existence. A lot of people have this meme of the Federal Reserve printing money — dollar bills getting kicked out — but that's not actually what happens. Dollars get created by being loaned into existence.

So the only way to increase the supply of dollars, which provides the liquidity to push it lower, is to also increase the size of the US dollar debt outstanding. You get a timing issue:

  • In the very short term, you get a lot of liquidity, which pushes the price down.
  • But at the very same time, you're creating a lot of demand because you're creating US dollar debt, which will eventually — but not immediately — be demand.

You get immediate supply increase and delayed demand. But eventually that demand shows up. When it does, the dollar strengthens and the whole world has to start scrambling for dollars.

下限的危险：DXY 跌到 80 会发生什么

If you get too far in the band to the downside — let's say the DXY falls to 80 — two things happen:

  1. An incredible amount of US dollar debt has been created (that's how the liquidity got in).
  2. Simultaneously, the euro goes up 20%, the yen goes up 20%, the pound goes up 20%.

When those other fiat currencies are rising, they're from countries that largely export goods. Their goods now get much more expensive. At some point importers stop buying from them. That puts pressure on those economies, which eventually turn down. And that happens right when all this new US dollar debt has been created.

Just as the dollar starts to strengthen, it strengthens against an incredible amount of new debt — you get a scramble for dollars, then the dollar goes higher, and can even go through the top end of the band.

上限的危险：违约与信贷紧缩

When you get through the top end of the band, you get a crisis. If it stays within the band, everybody can manage. But if you get too weak, too much dollar debt is created. If you get too strong, you start to have defaults and credit contraction. Under either scenario, bad things start to happen.

This is why you can use the dollar to understand where you're at in the cycle — why you could see copper, oil, gold, silver have 20-30-40-50% drawdowns even though the long-term thesis stays in play.

三、当前所处位置

Alex: Where are we at now in the band? What is the painful limit towards the extremity of that band? By the way, that's a great series — I would recommend Land Man to anyone. The first scene is magical. I think it's one of the best opening first scenes of any series I've ever watched.

Brent: Yeah, I agree, it's a great show — everybody should check it out.

Right now, for the last year we've been within this band. Markets have gone up, asset prices have been elevated, trade is happening, GDP has not been negative for many parts of the world. That's the sweet spot — 95, 96, 97 — even 90 would probably be even better for the whole world.

But when you start going higher, you start seeing pressure build. We're kind of at 101 now. This is still doable, not a crisis level, but if you start getting much higher, it's going to start putting pressure on the rest of the world.

四、地缘政治因素：伊朗冲突与黄金抛售

The other part of the reason the dollar has been strong really has to do with what happened when we were all in South Africa a few months ago. Literally when I was flying to see you on my way there is when the war in Iran kicked off. Initially on that very first day, gold and silver rose as a result of this conflict — but that was the high. Since then you've seen them sell off.

为什么黄金反而下跌

Part of the reason you've seen gold and silver sell off — specifically gold, not necessarily silver — is because countries around the world who have purchased gold as a reserve asset rather than holding treasuries are having to tap their reserves to get dollars to operate on the global stage.

This is a direct function of gold being an increasingly used reserve asset, because the system still runs on dollars. So when you get into a crisis, people who operate on the global stage don't sell dollars to get gold — they sell gold to get dollars to counteract the credit squeeze or the dollar scramble they're trying to get a hold of.

Over the last three or four months, you've seen Russia sell gold. You've seen Turkey sell gold. You've seen the Gulf States sell gold. That doesn't mean there aren't also central banks behind the scenes buying gold — I think they probably are.

黄金作为保险单

The reason gold is a good asset to own is because in a crisis it's very liquid. You can sell it. It's an insurance policy. When the crisis comes, you cash in your insurance policy so it can cover your losses. If gold is the insurance policy, gold gets sold because in that emergency, people need dollars.

If the system ever changes, perhaps that dynamic will change. But as of now, that's the dynamic. This is also why once the crisis passes and things start to liquefy again, gold typically rises the quickest coming out of a crisis. The crisis is over, you no longer have to sell it to get liquidity — liquidity is being provided by other means. That can go back into gold, pushing the price higher, and in many cases faster. But during the crisis itself, gold typically gets liquidated as an insurance policy.

五、反驳：中国的买入 vs. 抛售的央行

Alex: I want to push back a little on the central bank selling. Russia only sold about six tons. Turkey sold about 70, but it was mainly due to gold swaps with temporary liquidity operations for FX support. Kyrgyzstan maybe just one ton — very small sales. In the meantime, China actually loaded up on gold for the last six months. In May I think they had the largest purchase of about 170 tons, and they've been growing about 80% year-on-year on gold reserves.

A lot of people say gold is tanking, the peak has been reached — but in the meantime big central banks like China are actually loading up. What's your analysis?

干火药 vs. 受害者

Brent: I think that's right. As the price came down, they started to load up. The people who were prepared for the dollar to go up and gold to go down, prepared for some kind of crisis, and didn't have to go onto the global stage to buy oil — they can take advantage of the situation.

My point is that the people who are not ready for the crisis, who need liquidity in a crisis, have to become sellers. If you have another source of liquidity and dry powder — like China — you can go in and buy the dip. If you don't, you become a victim of it.

In that sense, it is the way gold took its more share as a reserve asset from the dollar, and thereby had to be used as a liquidity provider — to pay off dollar-denominated debt or just to scramble for dollars.

六、黄金价格的三大驱动因素

Alex: In your view, how did gold's price action change — if it's a pie chart of factors — from geopolitical, inflation hedge, and being an FX reserve?

Brent: I'd say:

  1. The debasement trade got way ahead of itself. It's not that it was incorrect, but it got way ahead of itself. By the end, both gold and silver were going straight up. Anytime you see a chart like that, it's going to come back down. Anybody who's been involved in markets for more than 10 years knows that when a chart goes straight up, it turns around at some point.

  2. Gold had to get liquidated during the initial parts of the Iran conflict because they needed liquidity. The dollar was stronger, energy prices were higher. Energy trades in dollars — as energy went up, you needed more dollars not only because of prices but because you needed to buy that energy.

  3. The recent hawkishness of Worsh coming in and saying "we're not necessarily going to cut rates as fast as everybody thinks" was a thing.

Maybe it's about equal on all three of those. But again, the whole point of the milkshake — of me talking about the dollar index — is not to convince people not to buy gold. It's actually the opposite.

长期看好，理解短期

I've said forever that gold was going to go to $5,000 — and it did. And I've said forever that gold will be the ultimate winner in all of this madness. But it can take a long time. In the meantime, understanding the dollar dynamics is very important.

The dollar getting stronger is probably the most bullish thing long-term for gold that there is. Because a strong dollar will destroy the global economy. The global system is not designed for a strong dollar. As the dollar gets stronger, it wrecks the monetary system. If the monetary system itself comes under pressure, then gold will probably raise its hand and say, "Hey, look at me. I'm a suitable solution — whether the government likes me or not."

Gold ultimately is the winner here. My work is not to dissuade people from owning gold — it's to help them understand why these drawdowns happen from time to time.

七、稳定币：Banana Split of Dollars

Alex: Now looking at recent stablecoin developments — with the GENIUS Act, there's been rulemaking that accelerated this development. A major joint proposal on customer identification programs, extending legislation and authorization of who issues stablecoins, and organizing the whole infrastructure behind it.

This is kind of what we discussed in the round table sessions — the birth of two dollars. It's the "banana split of dollars." Because of your milkshake theory, I want to bring in some other elements.

Brent: Yeah, that's a good one.

在岸美元 vs. 离岸美元

Alex: You have the onshore dollar — compliance stablecoins like USDC (Circle) — and the offshore one, USDT (Tether). How do you see the battle between the US-registered ones and the ones totally unregulated on a global basis outside the US? Could there be a price divergence between those two dollars?

Brent: The short answer is yes, I do think there could be a price divergence. The longer answer is it's nuanced — imminent and inevitable are not the same thing.

Right now the US government is largely letting these stablecoins proliferate because it reinforces the dollar as the currency used on the global stage. They're letting it run wild.

But within the US, they've said: here are strict rules with which you have to follow. If you're not a US-regulated entity, you have more leeway. The US can still influence it but not as much as a US-based company.

三种可能的价差来源

Down the road, you could have a price divergence for these reasons:

  1. Different backing. A US-regulated stablecoin has to be one-to-one backed by a US Treasury. A non-US-regulated stablecoin perhaps is only backed 80%, or is a combination of dollar, gold, copper, oil — some kind of basket. Or maybe it's algorithmic. So the peg could break.

  2. Different access. In the future, this is potentially a way the United States can weaponize the dollar. They could say: for US-regulated stablecoins, we are providing access to this specific Fed window, this specific Treasury program, this specific liquidity system. But if you are not a US company, you don't get that same liquidity.

  3. That's how you get the difference between an onshore dollar and an offshore dollar. The US will support and provide help to the onshore dollar; the offshore dollar is left to its own devices.

This is a way they can potentially in the future get more control over the Eurodollar market. Right now the US has more control over the Eurodollar market than any other entity, but not full control. These stablecoins are their attempt to build the new Eurodollar rails — in a way they have better control, visibility, and ability to manipulate or use as a tool of empire than is properly understood.

八、美国的新对外姿态：Painted Black

Alex: After Cape Town there was this episode where Scott Bessent on a panel was discussing how they grabbed wallets from the Iranian regime, and before the special operation in Iran they were coordinating financial chokepoints, isolating Iran from being able to transact. That gives an idea of where this could go.

"不再假装"的美国

Brent: The United States has changed the way it interacts with the rest of the world. You might say they haven't really changed — they've just stopped pretending otherwise. And that's fine, I'll accept that.

The point is, the United States in the last year or two has essentially said: "Listen, we are no longer doing things on a multilateral basis. We are no longer doing things for the greater good. We are no longer following the constraints of the rules-based order. Anything we do is going to be America First. If you don't like it, tough."

I know a lot of people don't like that, and that's fine. I don't particularly love it myself. But if you're investing your hard-earned money, it's probably best to deal with reality.

财政部战争

This doesn't mean the US can just do whatever it wants without ramifications. But this is a dramatically different way of publicly dealing with the rest of the world. The US Fed and Treasury have an enormous array of tools they can use within the monetary system to press their advantage.

There's a great book called Treasury's War that everybody should read. It lays out how this systematic ability was developed post-2001, post-9/11.

Don't underestimate their power. You've seen Bessent in the last six months: - Talk about causing a dollar shortage in Iran - Talk about using the dollar as a swap line in Argentina, not just to help Argentina but to accomplish geopolitical goals - Talk about confiscating wallets linked to the Iranian regime

These guys are very good at what they do. It's very easy to pretend they're idiots who don't know what they're doing, but you're putting yourself at a disadvantage if you believe that.

The Rolling Stones 隐喻

Going back to the paper we wrote — the other reason we called it The Band is that we used the Rolling Stones as a metaphor throughout. One of the most famous Rolling Stones songs is Paint It Black. In our "Painted Black" chapter, we talked about:

  • The willingness of the US to take off the white hat, to stop pretending they're the global hero, and just be the villain, for lack of a better way of saying it
  • The different ways they can weaponize the dollar
  • Their willingness to weaponize the dollar
  • Why it's so hard for the rest of the world to do anything about it

It's not that the rest of the world is helpless. It's not that the US can do whatever it wants. But this whole Eurodollar market — this is a prison. This is a US dollar prison the rest of the world built for themselves over the last 50 years. It's really, really hard to escape from it. Not impossible. It just means it's really hard to get out without a tremendous amount of pain. And we just haven't had that pain yet.

九、稳定币会取代欧洲美元市场吗？

Alex: Going back to the Eurodollar point — will stablecoins be within the Eurodollar market, or replace it fully? What about swap lines, SWIFT? What is the game?

平行建设，然后强制迁移

Brent: My guess is they're going to build it in parallel. Then at some point when it's to their advantage, they will say: "We are no longer operating in the traditional Eurodollar market. If you want access to US dollar funding, the Fed window, US markets — you're going to have to do it using these new rails."

I don't expect this next week. Probably not in the next couple of years. But a few years down the road, I think that could easily happen.

Swap Lines 的终结

Swap lines are another method where the US has traditionally offered liquidity to the rest of the world basically whenever they asked for it. I don't think that's going to be the case going forward.

The era of unlimited swap lines and swap lines without other strings attached are over. The US is going to demand allegiance, tribute, or some kind of concessions to get that liquidity in the future.

这届政府的关键人物

I think this administration knows how to do it:

  • Scott Bessent has already publicly acknowledged using two, three, four different methods of using the dollar as a weapon over the last year.
  • Stephen Miran talking about how the dollar can be used as a geopolitical tool.
  • Kevin Warsh who has come in now at the Fed. Both Warsh and Bessent have worked with Druckenmiller in the past. Druckenmiller and Soros were the ones who did the trade against the British pound when they broke the Bank of England. Scott Bessent was part of the team that did that.

You now have people who are very versed in capital markets, how they work, how they can be used to put pressure on other entities. Now you've got one of the guys who's done it in the private market sitting at the Treasury with his finger on the biggest button in the world.

I can't emphasize this enough. I don't know what's going to happen. I don't know exactly how they're going to play it out. But if you think they don't have some tricks up their sleeve, I would encourage you to think again.

十、其他阵营的反应：金砖国家

Alex: What are the secondary effects for other players — the Global South, the East, the BRICS+ countries? They're watching this and they're not just going to sit there and do nothing. They'll have to be part of that system somehow, at least for the beginning — until there might be a schism into two different monetary systems.

意愿高，能力低

Brent: They will undoubtedly try to do things. But it's important to remember they've been trying for 15 or 20 years already. They've made very little progress. It's extremely hard to do.

This is my whole point with the Eurodollar prison. It's not that people don't want to get out. It's not that they're not actively trying to saw through the bars. It's not that they're not calling for a lifeline. It's just really, really tough.

The desire to de-dollarize is extremely high. The ability to de-dollarize is extremely low — especially now that you have a US administration openly weaponizing the system against those trying to leave it.

美国也会痛，但相对更能承受

I want to be clear: this can't happen without tremendous amounts of pain — for the United States as well. There will be blowback, pushback, attempts to get out from under. Some will harm the US. But I think the US is better suited to handle that pressure than the rest of the world.

It's a relative game. Right now there is no good alternative.

十一、黄金作为替代方案的挑战

I'll make this point — this came up in South Africa. I think the best alternative to the US dollar system right now is gold. But gold is not a perfect system.

Michael and I had an argument with some of our fellow attendees who said gold could be used as the dollar by industry participants. We asked: do you think oil producers are going to sell their product for gold? That an oil producer is going to sell their energy and take gold in exchange rather than dollars, and just hold the gold?

Their point was: yeah, that's what they could do.

一个反例：25% 的下跌

Well, imagine if a couple of months ago these energy companies had sold their energy and taken gold, and the profits they otherwise would have made are now gone because gold has fallen 25% since. What do you think their quarterly earnings call would look like when they said, "Oh, by the way, we didn't sell our energy for dollars — we sold it for gold, and now gold is down 25%, and that's why our balance sheet is all messed up." What do you think the market reaction to their stock would be? I don't think it would be favorable.

It's not that they couldn't use gold. It's not that it wouldn't work. It's not easy, it's not perfect, and it can go against them. In the same ways the dollar system can hurt them, the gold system can hurt them too.

Let me say this before I'm done: this is not a reason not to own gold. Everybody should own gold. I'm a big believer in gold. I think gold's going to go a lot higher. But it does not mean it is the solution for everything. It's important to understand why it has challenges, and why the dollar is more ingrained than many people think.

十二、美联储与新主席 Kevin Warsh

Alex: I want to discuss what's happening at the Fed. We have a new Chairman, Kevin Warsh. He officially took office in May. In his first FOMC meeting last month, he kept rates steady, shortened the statements, and launched task forces on multiple things. They're also re-evaluating how to measure inflation with an averaged-out three-month inflation, if I understood correctly.

How do you see these early actions from Kevin Warsh affecting the potential for what we just discussed? Because I want to tie back in what you said — which I thought was interesting — that stablecoins will eventually replace the central bank of the United States, the Fed.

财政部 vs. 美联储

Brent: This is fascinating. I'll tell you what I think, but I don't know — because this is a very fluid situation.

For probably two or three years now, I've said we are moving towards a time where the Fed and the Treasury are going to come into conflict. If it comes to a big fight, the Treasury will win.

Part of the reason: as we're moving towards conflict with the rest of the world, moving towards an America First attitude as opposed to a greater-good Keynesian model, the central bank is one of the biggest tools/weapons/institutions the US can use against the rest of the world.

I've always thought that the idea of central bank independence was wrong. They have autonomy — they can operate within a certain bound — but if it's ever needed, the government will take away their independent charter.

Bessent × Warsh 的默契

With Scott Bessent sitting at Treasury, and he largely chose Warsh — Donald Trump was a little skeptical of Warsh, liked him but was skeptical. I think he outsourced that decision to Treasury because he wanted a Fed that would cooperate. I've got to believe Bessent and Warsh had a lot of conversations before he was confirmed. Warsh is probably on board with whatever Treasury wants to do, or at least mostly in agreement.

稳定币赋予财政部的新工具

One of the reasons I've always thought the Fed would eventually lose in a big battle with Treasury: stablecoins to a certain extent give the Treasury a tool they never had before.

The Fed has always been the Treasury's bank. Treasury is not a bank — it doesn't have its own banking operations. The Fed is the Treasury's bank. But with stablecoins, you don't really need a bank. Banks have been the distribution system between the government and the public. With stablecoins, crypto, digital assets, and everyone having a smartphone, it's not easy — but you can skip the whole distribution system and go directly to the customer.

That gives Treasury an advantage over the Fed. So I believe the Fed and Treasury are now going to work in harmony more than they have over the last five to ten years.

Warsh 的偏好 vs. 现实约束

What you've heard from Warsh so far is what he would like to do — that doesn't necessarily mean he'll be able to. At the end of the day, the central bank is there to support markets. That's why they were invented. They are the lender of last resort. The idea that we're going into some big market downturn and he's not going to cut rates or do QE or stimulus is wrong. If it comes to that, of course he will do it — that's why he's there.

But if markets hold up longer than expected, he's hoping to implement these more hawkish policies before having to pivot to dovish. I don't know whether that happens this year or next.

There are way too many people that are certain about this topic. Better to just understand what the possibilities are: - People certain he'll remain hawkish should not be so certain. - People certain he'll go back to QE in the next few months because "he's secretly a big dove" — that's also a wrong certainty.

He will do it if forced. But he doesn't want to, and he won't willingly do it at the first sign of trouble. I'm open to being proven wrong.

Next month's meeting is going to be very important. That will tell the tale more than this last one. This last one, it was going to be very hard for him to come out in the very first meeting and cut rates — that probably would not have sent a great signal. He felt markets are strong enough that he can get to the next meeting before making some big decision. The July meeting is much more important than the June meeting.

十三、商业银行如何应对：稳定币战争

Alex: It's fascinating because it touches the foundation of how those institutions work. It also begs the question of how commercial banks would react. Stablecoins can only get the yield if they're a bank — Kraken, for example, one of the oldest crypto/Bitcoin exchanges, is now a bank. It just got a license, being regulated like a bank, so they can issue those stablecoins. It changes the nature of how the banking system works.

三场并行的战争

Brent: This is a big deal. We wrote another paper called Stablecoin Wars. There are stablecoin wars going on: - Between different issuers - Between countries - Between the banking system and the stablecoin issuers

This is a huge deal. Part of the reason the war is so viciously fought is that the profits are enormous.

收益率之争

Right now, stablecoins cannot pay a yield — by definition. That's largely determined by the banking system saying: we don't want them to pay a yield, because if they do, deposits will flee banks and go to stablecoin issuers. Banks don't want that.

But eventually it's going to migrate that way. Banks are either going to have to issue their own stablecoins, or see their deposits leave.

小银行的困境

This is going to be particularly troublesome for smaller banks and community-sized banks. The big JP Morgans, Wells Fargo, Bank of Americas will probably be okay. But smaller banks — it's just another thing they have to deal with, and probably won't be able to. As a result, I think they're going to get swallowed up by bigger banks or go out of business.

The more that happens, the stronger the Treasury's hand gets.

全球需求：不为收益，为价值稳定

Even if stablecoins are not allowed to pay interest, I still think they're going to proliferate greatly. The rest of the world isn't holding stablecoins for the yield. Americans largely look for yield on their deposits. The rest of the world just wants to hold dollars.

They're not competing against perfection — they're competing against their local currencies. They're trying to get something better than their local currency. If they can hold a US dollar balance that doesn't lose value as quickly as their local currency, they'll hold US dollars all day long. We're seeing that as the market size of stablecoins continues to grow.

十四、稳定币技术底层：网络之争

Alex: It's about 300 billion at the moment. If it's about to replace or largely replace the Eurodollar market — which is multiple trillions — there's a big vacuum still to fill.

One thing we didn't discuss: stablecoins are cryptocurrencies, and depending on the network there are different ways they can be sent. One of the most popular is Ethereum, Tron. What are the vulnerabilities? Those technologies are not perfect. It's been six, seven, eight years that I'm busy with cryptocurrencies. For every cryptocurrency there's an advantage and a disadvantage — you always have to compromise on scale, traceability, anonymity, or efficiency. What do you think is going to take that big pie? The Trons and Ethereums we already see? Solana? What's your view?

让市场先跑，再收编

Brent: I think that is probably right. The US administration is just going to let stablecoins proliferate. Once this is really ingrained in the whole world, then they will go to maybe the top two or three networks and say: "OK, you now work for us. Or else this is going to happen." Or perhaps behind the scenes they're building their own network — I don't know.

They're letting the free market run amok to a certain extent because it benefits them in the short term. But at some point, I think they're going to mandate: if you want access to Fed liquidity, US Treasury, US markets — you're going to need to do it on these rails, with this token, under these circumstances.

I don't know exactly how or when. But Tron, Ethereum are probably the best easy way to get exposure to this thesis.

十五、结语：诚实面对目标

Alex: We covered a lot of ground on this one. Is there anything else you think would be interesting to share with the viewers relating to this topic?

为什么理解美元很重要

Brent: People have to be very honest with themselves about what they're trying to achieve. Most of the time when I come on these podcasts, it's investment-related or has to do with money. People listen because they want to protect their money and grow their money.

The reason I've pounded the table on the dollar is not because I think the dollar is a great currency, and not because I think you should sell your gold and just sit in dollars. That's not the point.

The point is that the dollar is one of the biggest variables in global markets. To understand macro, geopolitics, capital flows, and asset prices, you need to understand the dollar. As much as we might all dislike it, it's a very important component. Once you understand how the dollar system works, it's easier to understand what's happening in those markets — and hopefully that translates into better returns, sleeping better at night, and less anxiety about your portfolio.

For those who don't think it's important to understand fiat currencies and how they trade relative to each other — I would encourage you not to be of that view.

不要过度确定

The other thing I would say: I meet a lot of people that are very certain on their thesis. Bitcoin is the answer. Gold is the answer. Equities are the answer. Uranium is the answer. All of those things are very important. But don't be so certain of your position that you're closed off to ideas that may run counter to it.

Try to be prepared for everything rather than just being certain about everything.

收尾

Alex: Great. Thanks a lot for your time, Brent. For the viewers and listeners, Brent is very active on X at @SantiagoAuFund. This will appear on the screen. You also have your newsletter and your website on which you publish your reports, articles, and thoughts — this will all be linked in the description. Thank you Brent, and I hope to follow up with you very soon on this topic.

Brent: Thanks for having me. And stay cool over there in Europe.

Alex: I will.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：黄金下跌恰恰证明了它作为"储备资产"的成功
多数分析者把最近几个月黄金回调解读为"debasement trade 失败"，Brent 却揭示了一个被完全忽视的镜像逻辑：**黄金正因为"太成功"而下跌**。当俄罗斯、土耳其、海湾国家被迫抛售黄金去换美元，这不是黄金失宠的证据，而是黄金已经**替代了美国国债的储备地位**的证据——只有真正被当作储备资产的东西，才会在危机中被首先动用。
换句话说：**黄金的下跌，是它取代美债过程中的"服役伤"，不是"退役信号"。** 这与保险公司的资产负债表逻辑完全同构——保险公司越是被大量理赔，越说明它的产品被信任、被广泛持有。危机中被抛售的资产不是失败者，而是流动性最好的赢家。
## 二、跨领域连接：石油"价格带"与货币"价格带"的同构——一个热力学隐喻
Brent 借用《Land Man》里油价"带状区间"的比喻是精彩的，但更深的洞察在于：**美元指数的"band"其实是一个耗散结构**，就像生物体的体温调节——37°C 上下 2°C 是舒适区，超过则触发系统性反馈。
这里的意外连接是：**美元的机制类似于抗生素的

item.title: [Lex Clips] Why the Roman Empire collapsed (in the West) | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

罗马西部崩溃的"熵增陷阱"——一个反常识的系统性观察

大多数叙事把西罗马的崩溃归因于"蛮族入侵"，但转录中Kaldellis揭示的真相更冷酷：崩溃不是被外力打垮的，而是被自身的反馈回路吃掉的。每失去一个行省，就失去一份税基；税基一少，军队就更弱；军队一弱，下一波入侵就更难压制。这不是历史学，这是耗散系统的热力学——一个原本靠资源循环维持的复杂结构，一旦某个回路断裂，就会沿着"负反馈失效→正反馈自噬"的路径滑向不可逆的坍缩。有趣的是，这与生物学中的"器官衰竭级联"（一个器官罢工，其他器官相继失灵）在数学结构上完全同构。罗马西部不是"死于"蛮族，而是死于自身丧失了自我修复的最低能量阈值。

意外观察：地理学作为"隐形执政官"

这段访谈里最颠覆性的一句话被轻描淡写地带过了——"东部不是主动挽救自己，而是地理把入侵者推向了西方"。这意味着历史上被称颂为"东罗马坚韧智慧"的那部分，其实有相当比例是博斯普鲁斯海峡这条水道在替人类做决策。哥特人没有舰队，所以他们只能顺着大陆向西流。这让我想到现代硅谷的崛起——很多人归因于"文化"或"精英"，但真正的底层原因可能只是加州的气候允许全年户外原型测试、以及斯坦福恰好在铁路时代的土地馈赠上。当我们把制度的胜利归于"人的选择"时，往往是在为地形学的沉默功劳颁奖。

跨领域连接：从"首都反转"看今天的注意力经济

Kaldellis描述了一个惊人的倒转：色雷斯——早期罗马人眼中"帝国的腋窝"——最终成为罗马世界的心脏，而罗马城本身变成了"狼在广场上游荡"的乡下小镇。中心和边缘的身份，是可以在两三百年内彻底互换的。

把这个模型套到当代：底特律曾是20世纪的"罗马"，硅谷曾是果园。而今天，我们正处在下一次"君士坦丁堡时刻"——注意力资源正在从传统媒体（旧罗马）向创作者经济、AI界面（新君士坦丁堡）迁移，但大多数人仍在旧首都里争夺荣誉。历史给出的残酷提示是：当一个中心开始需要"重新征服"曾经属于自己的边缘时，它就已经成为边缘了。Justinian重新征服罗马城，就像今天的老牌媒体巨头收购某个TikTok爆款账号——这不是复兴，而是承认。

隐藏的系统性真相

罗马法今天以"罗马"的名义流传全球，但我们手中的《民法大全》其实是君士坦丁堡的版本。这暗示了一条冷酷的元规律：一个文明的"品牌"往往在它的地理原点消亡之后才达到最大流通量。就像"瑞士军刀"的军事根源早已不重要，"罗马"这个词的力量在罗马城衰败后反而膨胀。这提醒我们审视自己所在的组织、公司、国家——你所忠诚的那个名字，可能早已不在它原来的地方，而在另一个你尚未察觉的"色雷斯"里悄然重建。

📝 转录文本

问题的提出：西罗马为何崩溃？

As we talk about diverting resources, uh, this slowly the West declines and collapses in uh 476 AD. So, the Visigoths sack Rome in 410 AD. And in 476 AD, the last Western emperor is deposed, marking the end of the Western Roman Empire.

High-level question: what is the reason uh for that decline and the collapse of the Western Roman Empire? So — non-stop civil wars, as we've talked about; weak emperors; uh there's an economic money crisis, so shrinking tax base, rising army costs; uh there was a loss of key provinces, especially North Africa; reliance on contracted barbarian troops, as they immigrated more and more into uh the Western Roman Empire; cascading invasions, migrations amplified by Hun pressure; and then the East-West split — the richer East survives, the West can't stabilize under that split.

恶性循环：资源流失的连锁反应

The crux of the matter is that barbarian armies — so, armies from outside the empire — put increasing pressure on it um during the 5th century. And for whatever reason, when the Roman state failed to sort of suppress one of them and say settled them here, or allowed them to have here, or couldn't stop them and they took over a province — like the Vandals did in North Africa — this created this kind of vicious cycle.

Because then the Roman state lost access to the resources from those provinces, that is the taxes, and without those resources, they couldn't uh, you know, maintain, mobilize, pay for their armies. Then they had [clears throat] fewer armies to handle the next wave of barbarians that came in. The previous barbarians who settled kind of declared their kind of independence. They also could not be put down, and so this created a vicious cycle that just continued in that way.

It's not rocket science, actually. I mean, you can see it playing out.

Yeah.

Now, who these groups are, where they came from, why did they cross — those are different questions, right? But from the Roman standpoint, that's how it was experienced. These are armies that can defeat a Roman field army, right, in battle. Um, and sometimes they do. Um, and they then settle and take over territories, and then you lose the revenues, and so you can't recruit your own armies, and so anyway, that's the kind of cycle.

东部的援助与哥特人的冲击

Um, the East tried to help. Occasionally, they would send forces, but they were also subject to some of the same pressures. Cuz remember, the Goths crossed the Danube um for the first time in a major way um in 376. Then you have the Battle of Adrianople a couple years later, where like one and a half field armies is just annihilated, and the emperor Valens dies, and the Goths are just like kind of loose in the Balkans.

Um and there was then a long war with them — 4 years. It results in a kind of truce, where neither side wins, and the Romans for the first time accept the presence of this quasi-independent Gothic army in the territory of the empire. Uh, this is unprecedented, as I said, and it creates a lot of tensions and dysfunctions.

地理为何拯救了东部

Okay. So, why does the East survive and the West does not, right? There's — so, there's lots of reasons that we can give here, but one of them is the geography that we were talking about earlier.

In other words, without a fleet, which they don't have, the Goths can't cross over from Thrace or the western Balkans to Asia Minor and go plunder some of the more prosperous provinces. So, they're stuck in the Balkans. They're gradually pushed kind of in toward the western Balkans, which is like the worst part — you you know at the time — like you want to be in. And eventually they just decide, well, you know, it's kind of nicer in Naples. And so they go to Italy, right? Just because there's just a lot more resources there for them.

And that's one way that the East just kind of manages to — it doesn't do this deliberately, but the geography kind of pushes these invaders to the west.

So, that's one — so, geography is some of it's sort of the luck of geography and —

Yeah.

both of the invaders and the geography of the empire.

Yeah.

But, you know, the East had a lot of invaders since —

Yes.

and was able to still barely at times survive the invaders. So, and some of that could be luck cuz some of it could be about the nature of the invaders and the effectiveness of their military, all that kind of stuff.

君士坦丁堡：不可攻破的指挥中心

By the way, it worked in the other direction, too. So, if you have like Persians or Arabs coming into Asia Minor from the East, they usually can't cross over into your Balkan provinces. So, you can fall back on one side or the other side. If you fortified Constantinople so that it's an impregnable bunker, which it was, right? So, you're holding your command center in Constantinople, you can alternate between your European or your Asian provinces depending on where the trouble is. You can call in reinforcements from either side.

So, the East does have that kind of advantage. This is not the reason why it survives for as long as it does, though. It is a reason. You know, also they acquire flamethrowers at some point, which those help, too.

Yeah.

真正的答案：地方社区的选择

Um, right? Ultimately, I think it's the decision made by local communities to stick with the Roman state, like ultimately. Like they're not going to actively side with invaders. And when the trouble passes, they restore community ties with Constantinople immediately. Like this is their natural political home. Um and — and that's the question that I'm really trying to answer. Like why does that happen?

You know, we should say — just just like we've articulated multiple times — that the Roman Empire survives. Uh so it's not like the Roman Empire collapsed and then another Byzantine Empire was born. Really, there is a shift that happens.

And part of that shift is because of Constantine. Right, it's not just about where the capital is, but I'm sure that there is just a general move towards that direction from the west to the east. And in many ways, you know, weak emperors and — and the west, weak leaders, um, the structure of government, all these kinds of elements put together, maybe demographic shifts and declines that have to do with the fact that the center of the empire has moved.

That's right.

And so ultimately, it's not that the West Roman Empire collapsed, it is that the center of the Roman Empire shifted. And because of that, the historic West collapsed.

罗马法的悖论：中心的完全倒转

It's quite striking. So for example, already by like in the 430s, Constantinople is basically codifying Roman law for the west. So Roman law in Latin is codified in Constantinople and then sent to the west as a — "Oh, here's your law code." And this is in the 430s, so after the alleged division between the two, which, like I said, is kind of loose — it's not hard and fast.

In the 6th century, it is the east that reconquers part of the west under Justinian. And not only that, he also recodifies Roman law on a much bigger scale, and then that law becomes Western law. Uh so, in a certain sense, Roman law as we know it comes from Constantinople. Doesn't come from ancient Rome. I mean, ultimately it comes from ancient Rome, but we don't have those versions of it. We have the East Roman versions of it.

Um, it is East Rome that reconquers Rome. This is a funny inversion, you know, because Constantinople is situated in a province, Thrace, which always was sort of considered backwards. Right? In the time of like the early Caesars, if you were in Rome and you thought, you know, Thrace, it was like, "Ugh." It's like, you know, some sort of — yeah, armpit of the Roman Empire or something, you know?

But by Justinian's time, it's a complete inversion. Like the capital of the Roman world, the heart of it, is there, and Rome is a kind of backwater provincial city in Italy, you know, that you've just liberated from the Goths, and it's kind of in ruins. There were wolves roaming the streets of the forum. So, this is a complete o- o- you know, overhaul of the centers of power. It's turned inside out almost.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] What If AI Proves the Riemann Hypothesis and No One Understands It? – @3blue1brown
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的隐喻结构：这段对话其实是在讨论"知识的运输成本"

表面上他们在讨论"AI 证明黎曼猜想我们能不能看懂"，但真正的底层问题其实是知识的可压缩性。三种解题路径——闪电（lightning bolt）、造山（mountain building）、蛮力（raw hustle）——本质上对应了三种不同的信息熵：

  • 闪电式：两个已知语义节点之间的低熵连接，人类几乎零成本吸收；
  • 造山式：需要在概念空间中新建一整片地形，运输成本 = 建造成本；
  • 蛮力式：不可压缩的长链推理，运输成本 ≈ 无穷。

一个让人意外的观察是：Grothendieck、望月新一这类"造山者"其实早就制造过"AI 式的理解危机"。也就是说，"AI 让人类看不懂数学"这个恐惧，人类数学家自己已经间歇性地施加给同行几十年了。ABC 猜想的困境，本质上是一次"生物大脑跑出的 AlphaProof"。

二、跨领域连接：这三种解法结构与生物进化中的三种适应模式惊人对应

  • 闪电式 ↔ 水平基因转移（细菌之间瞬时借用抗药基因）：低成本、瞬时融合两个已成熟系统。
  • 造山式 ↔ 寒武纪大爆发：新的身体计划（body plan）出现，整个生态需要重新学习如何与它共存。
  • 蛮力式 ↔ 中性突变的漂变积累：单个突变看不出方向，只有极长时间尺度上才能看到功能涌现。

这暗示了一个反直觉的判断：AI 数学的"可理解性"不是一个技术问题，而是一个生态位问题。人类能吸收闪电，能勉强消化造山（花几十年），但对蛮力式几乎无免疫力——就像多细胞生物无法在细菌尺度上追踪基因漂变一样。

三、更深的一层：真正的风险不是"看不懂"，而是"看错了却以为看懂了"

Dwarkesh 和嘉宾都在担忧 AI 会不会给出人类无法验证的证明。但 ABC 猜想的教训指向一个更尖锐的方向：望月的证明有人类可读的符号，却依然不可验证。也就是说——

可读性 ≠ 可验证性。人类习惯把"能顺着读下去"当作"能检查对错"，但这两件事在高维抽象中会脱钩。

这带来一个几乎没人提的推论：AI 时代数学界最稀缺的角色，不会是"证明者"，也不会是"翻译者"，而是"信任的仲裁者"——一种介于数学家、逻辑学家、社会学家之间的新职业。它类似于中世纪教会中的"神学审议委员会"：不是产生新知识，而是判定一段陈述可否被共同体接纳为"我们知道的事情"。

换句话说，AI 不会摧毁数学，但会迫使数学第一次直面它一直回避的问题："我们知道"到底是一个逻辑事实，还是一个社会协议？

📝 转录文本

提问：AI 若解开黎曼猜想，人类的理解会同步增长吗？

People have this worry about mathematics in particular that, you know, AIs will prove the Riemann hypothesis and our understanding of mathematics won't be any the better for it. I have a couple of questions about this.

The first one is whether this is like a thing you should expect. Like, isn't the reason humans come up with general natural objects and subgoals and whatever when we're working on a big problem is that it's just useful when you're trying to work on the complicated important problem? And so we can just think about, like, theoretically, would this even be a simpler way to solve the Riemann hypothesis as opposed to just coming up with the natural abstractions that are relevant to thinking about the problem?

And then two, empirically, is this what we observe when AIs do make progress on problems today? When the AI came up with that counterexample to the unit distance problem conjecture, you can just read its chain of thought and — it's not understandable to me cuz I don't know anything about mathematics, but it seems to other mathematicians it was understandable, and it made use of known concepts in mathematics and proved relationships, stating them in all the natural language. And as a result, accelerated our understanding of the connection between this object and this conjecture.

So, is this even a thing empirically, or is this a thing we should be worried about?

回应：三种可能的"解法形态"

I think it depends on the nature of — yeah, like again, if we sort of break down the three possible ways of solving the Riemann hypothesis.

1. 闪电式（Lightning Bolt）：跨领域的小想法

That one, and all the other big one from this year — was the like a certain Erdős problem numbered like 1196, but it's about these things called primitive sets. But basically, it had that character of bringing an idea from a seemingly different field.

As soon as you just present the basic idea to a mathematician, you say like, "Hm, what if we use this — like try to Markov chain process where we show that this thing is one from the bottom up probabilistically rather than the top down, and like use the van Mangoldt function." If you say that to someone in the know, they'd kind of know how to run with it.

So it's very like a small idea that has the form of: expertise in one field, expertise in another, draw a little lightning bolt between them. Those are going to be very human-parsable, right? Cuz all you have to do is just show the start and end point of what those connections are.

2. 造山式（Mountain Building）：新建理论地形

If the character of it is mountain building, you do have to put in a lot more time to understand that new mountain that was built, because it's like a new threat. That's not just a lightning bolt between them.

3. 蛮力式（Raw Hustle）：超长推理链

And then if the nature of the progress was just raw hustle, right? It's just this super long thing — there's no new theories, but it's just like a long long long chain of reasoning answer — then you would have that where, okay, there's this whole digestion process.

关键判断：并无单一答案

So I don't think there's one clear answer. I think it depends on what the solution there would look like.

And on the mountain building side, it would actually be really interesting to see: is it by default a very human-understandable — like the way that we see new theories from great mathematicians? Or is it like an alien different kind of mountain being built, where we even have to reprocess the kinds of abstractions that we engage with?

追问：ABC 猜想的前车之鉴

Right. Well, the closest example here would be, you know, the attempted solution of the ABC conjecture.

Lecture — that was, we maybe shouldn't get into that one, but... it's probably not a correct solution. But basically, it's this whole new way of thinking that this otherwise reputable mathematician in Japan had come up with. And it just took mathematicians a long, long time to even parse what he was saying. But it had the feeling of just like an alien bit of mathematics that's theory building. It's not just a long chain of reasoning.

He called it like inter-universal geometry or something.

最大的恐惧：登山之后发现山是错的

And so the fear that you would have is that, yeah — like, does that... the biggest fear would be that it does that, and then much like the ABC conjecture, people work for years to go up the mountain, and then — dang it — this just isn't right, right? And if it turns out to be wrong, but it really looked right...

But even if it was right, there's just a lot of effort to hike up a new mountain.

Yeah. If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的隐喻结构：这段对话其实是在讨论"知识的运输成本"
表面上他们在讨论"AI 证明黎曼猜想我们能不能看懂"，但真正的底层问题其实是**知识的可压缩性**。三种解题路径——闪电（lightning bolt）、造山（mountain building）、蛮力（raw hustle）——本质上对应了三种不同的**信息熵**：
- 闪电式：两个已知语义节点之间的低熵连接，人类几乎零成本吸收； - 造山式：需要在概念空间中新建一整片地形，运输成本 = 建造成本； - 蛮力式：不可压缩的长链推理，运输成本 ≈ 无穷。
一个让人意外的观察是：**Grothendieck、望月新一这类"造山者"其实早就制造过"AI 式的理解危机"**。也就是说，"AI 让人类看不懂数学"这个恐惧，人类数学家自己已经间歇性地施加给同行几十年了。ABC 猜想的困境，本质上是一次"生物大脑跑出的 AlphaProof"。
## 二、跨领域连接：这三种解法结构与生物进化中的三种适应模式惊人对应
- **闪电式** ↔ **水平基因转移**（细菌之间瞬时借用抗药基因）：低成本、瞬时融合两个已成熟系统。

item.title: [Lex Clips] Is religion good or bad for human civilization? | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、殉道者日历：一个帝国把"自我矛盾"制度化的奇观

最出人意料的观察藏在 Kaldellis 那句轻描淡写里——东罗马人几乎每周都在教堂里纪念被罗马皇帝屠杀的殉道者。想一想这意味着什么：一个自称"罗马 + 基督徒"的社会，把"罗马皇帝迫害基督徒"这段血史编入了年度礼仪循环。这不是遗忘，也不是和解，而是把内部张力制度化为集体记忆的心跳。

这在系统论上是极罕见的稳定策略。多数政权处理身份矛盾的方式是压抑或改写（如苏联的"人民的敌人"清单），而拜占庭选择了周期性地把矛盾拿出来展演。这更像是心理治疗里的"暴露疗法"，或者软件工程里的 chaos engineering——主动、有节律地重放系统的失败案例，反而让系统更耐久。当你的宗教仪式每周提醒你"罗马身份≠基督徒身份"，你反而不需要为二者的合一而焦虑，因为你已经承认它们只是叠加态。

二、"三元实验室"作为一种反脆弱的身份架构

Kaldellis 用了 "laboratory" 这个词——罗马 + 基督徒 + 希腊三种元素在同一躯体内共存。这句话如果不深挖会被当成人文抒情，但它其实描述了一种极其罕见的组合数学结构：三个元素两两独立可辨（存在"非罗马的基督徒"、"非基督徒的罗马人"、"非希腊语的罗马人"），却在同一社会里稳定共存上千年。

跨领域连接一下：这非常像生态学里的"niche partitioning"（生态位分割）。同一片森林能容纳猫头鹰、狐狸、鹰共存，不是因为它们相同，而是因为每个物种都在略微不同的时间/空间维度上取食。拜占庭社会里，一个人可以"全力做希腊学者"，另一个可以"全力做修士"，第三个可以"只做政客不在乎宗教"——帝国不是要求所有人合一，而是提供三种独立的荣誉阶梯。

这带来一个惊人的推论：拜占庭之所以能撑一千年，可能恰恰因为它内部不是"一个身份"而是"三个可切换模式"。任何单一身份系统在遇到危机时会整体崩塌，但一个三元系统在其中一维受创时，可以退守到另外两维。1204 年拉丁人攻陷君士坦丁堡后，帝国靠"希腊语文化 + 正教信仰"两条腿在流亡中重生——罗马那一维暂时失灵，另两维顶上了。

三、宁选苏丹不选教皇：一个反直觉的"成本-价值"计算

在帝国末年选择"宁可被土耳其人统治也不接受天主教"这一细节，用现代眼光看几乎是自毁——为什么放弃基督教盟友，投向异教征服者？

Kaldellis 给出了一个冷静的解释：土耳其人只要税和顺从，不要求你改信；教皇要求你改信。所以真正的成本函数不是"政治独立性"，而是"身份保真度"。

这里有一个让人不安的跨领域投射：这个决策模型今天仍在无声运作。想想看—— - 为什么许多小国宁愿被大国经济控制，也不愿加入"要求价值观趋同"的联盟？ - 为什么某些技术团队宁愿使用性能更差的开源方案，也不接受性能更好但"生态锁定"的商业方案？ - 为什么用户会离开功能更强但要求"你必须成为我们社区一员"的产品？

"外部剥削"往往比"内部同化"更容易被接受，因为前者只索取资源，后者索取自我。拜占庭人在 15 世纪就用国运做了这个实验，而我们至今在个人、组织、国家的每一层重复它。

四、隐藏的方法论警告

最后一个让我停下来的细节：Kaldellis 反复强调他作为历史学家"要尝试把这些元素当作彼此独立来测试模型"。这不是学术套话，而是认识论上的一次自我校准——他知道"一切都被宗教解释"的诱惑有多大，正因如此他刻意把宗教从默认变量里剔除，看看其他部分能不能独立立住。

这其实是一条通用的系统分析原则：当一个变量看起来解释一切时，它可能什么也没解释。就像"进化"在生物学、"权力"在福柯之后的社会理论、"注意力机制"在当代 AI 论文里的境遇——过度解释力等于零解释力。Kaldellis 对"宗教"的方法论警觉，值得任何研究者借鉴到自己领域里那个"过于好用"的概念上。

📝 转录文本

一、无法回避的问题：宗教对帝国是建设性还是破坏性？

Lex： The impossible question — and I apologize for asking it, but maybe it's just worthwhile to try to think about it. On the grand scale of centuries of the East Roman Empire, is religion a constructive or a destructive force for the empire? Did it help it flourish, or did it hold it back?

二、正教与文化：难以拆解的共生体

Kaldellis： This society identified itself increasingly with what it saw as orthodoxy. Orthodoxy just means correct belief. I mean, like, every religion ultimately thinks it's orthodox, right?

Lex： [laughter]

Kaldellis： But that's the conventional name, right? Orthodoxy today might be called Greek Orthodoxy, and it's such an essential part of the culture. It really is. I can't think of the two of them inde— I mean, I try to, because as a historian I have to test the models, you know, and try to see these things as independent of each other. But ultimately it's very difficult to disentangle them.

And I would say that, to the degree that this is a successful society historically, its religion definitely contributed to that and played a role in it. Not without cost, right? It mattered to them. They valued it, but things that matter to you have a cost. And they paid that price. They were willing to pay that price for it.

三、末代的抉择：宁选苏丹，不选教皇

For example, at the very end they had to choose between Catholicism — Western rule, where they essentially have to compromise on their faith — or rule by Muslims, the Turks, where they would not have to compromise their faith. And some of them were explicit that they were preferring option B, because "the Turks will at least respect our identity." Like, that's a cost.

Lex： Yeah.

四、税收与治理：宗教之下的世俗引擎

Kaldellis： But there's other layers, like taxation and also the functions of government —

Lex： Yeah, yeah.

Kaldellis： — that are connected to religion to the degree it interacts with the tax system. But it feels like that's the engine of the society, and religion is more like the cultural layer.

Kaldellis： Yeah, so this is why I said that I try, in thinking of the models that we use to understand the society, not to assume that religion is everywhere and for everything. Now, I have colleagues who do that and I would disagree with them about this, but you're exactly right. There are elements of this society that I like to see as their own thing — like the Roman identity, for example. It's not the same as Orthodoxy.

But also, the operations of the state — those are very pragmatic things, and you see them intersect with religion largely in areas of terminology, or the vocabulary that they use, or, you know, like at the top, but not at their actual functioning. So, in my field, I'm a historian who's tried to promote other aspects of the culture as having a kind of integrity of their own, and not to subsume them all within Orthodoxy.

五、"你是罗马人，还是基督徒？"—— 一个不允许"两者都是"的追问

Lex： So, I mean, again, an impossible way of asking the same question: if you asked a Roman — a million Romans across a thousand years — "are you a Roman or a Christian?", what would be the answer for that?

Kaldellis： Oh, absolutely.

Lex： You don't get to say both. You're not allowed to do that.

Kaldellis： Why not? It's the true answer.

Lex： Okay. So you're saying they're really tied together.

六、逻辑上的分离：殉道者与异教罗马人的记忆

Kaldellis： Well, they overlap. But if you were to — and this might be a somewhat more subtle conversation than most of them would want to have — but if you were to say, "is Roman and Christian the same thing to you?", if they thought about it for a little while, they'd quickly be able to say no. Because, I mean, just logically, there were Christians who were not Romans.

Lex： [clears throat]

Kaldellis： The Bulgarians, or the Rus, or, you know, whoever. There were lots of Christians who were not Roman, and they did not regard them as Roman just because they were Christian.

Conversely, they knew perfectly well that there had been Romans who were not Christians. And they knew this how? Because in the churches, like almost every week, they celebrated martyrs who had been killed by the Roman emperors in the past. And so it was like the performance of violence by Roman emperors against Christians was like something they remember. They knew perfectly well.

So those two things did not overlap perfectly.

七、身份的模式切换：从政客到修士到希腊学者

And there were some contexts in which you would be more Roman, and there were some contexts in which you would be more Christian. And you find characters who — and I love to do this — I find characters who, as some sources say, wouldn't pay too much attention to Christian things. Like, they're just politicians, they were just doing their work. And then you find characters who devote themselves — they go all in on orthodoxy and monasticism — and they just don't care about the Roman stuff at all.

And that is, in fact, part of the reason why I like this society: because it has these kinds of options for people to choose.

And there's another one — there's the Greek stuff, too. Because their language is Greek, their literature is Greek. And so there are people in the society who go all in on the Greek stuff, right?

八、三元实验室：历史上独一无二的组合

I see this society as this kind of laboratory of these three elements coexisting in these fascinating combinations. Usually it's combinations, right? But sometimes you'll find people who are, like, kind of trying to be purists in one way or another. And it's the only society in history in which you can do this — because it's the only society that's Roman and Christian and Greek. But in different ways, right?

九、反事实的诱惑与不可能的分析

Lex： Yeah. Unfortunately, I think it's tempting, but it's probably impossible, to say: if we remove the taxation system, would the Roman Empire be more or less successful? If we removed religion, would it be more or less successful? If we removed Greek and kept it Latin all the way through, would it be more or less successful? It's probably impossible to do this kind of analysis, because they're all so deeply integrated.

Kaldellis： And fundamentally, it also wouldn't be what it was. It would be something different.

十、核心之问：为什么它能持续这么久？

Lex： But there is a big temptation — and this is at the core of your work — to understand: why did the East Roman Empire last as long as it did? And why did the broader Roman Empire last as long as it did? Because it is one of, if not the most, successful empires in terms of duration and stability, in a very dense part of the world.

观看原视频 →

最出人意料的观察藏在 Kaldellis 那句轻描淡写里——**东罗马人几乎每周都在教堂里纪念被罗马皇帝屠杀的殉道者**。想一想这意味着什么：一个自称"罗马 + 基督徒"的社会，把"罗马皇帝迫害基督徒"这段血史编入了年度礼仪循环。这不是遗忘，也不是和解，而是**把内部张力制度化为集体记忆的心跳**。
